source.title: Performance Regression Policy & Thresholds (Company Standard)
source.author: Allison Grant
source.body:
## Overview
This page defines Redwood Inference’s company-wide standard for:
- What counts as a performance regression vs. measurement noise.
- Minimum sample sizes and repeatability requirements.
- Pass/fail thresholds used by automated perf gating (PR checks, nightly canaries, and release-branch gates).
- Required artifacts and triage expectations when a regression is detected.

This policy is designed to support the “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” program and applies broadly to the serving runtime, kernels, scheduler/batching, and benchmark harness changes.

**Owner:** Observability (Engineering) in partnership with Serving Runtime and SRE.

---

## Scope
### In scope
This policy applies to any change that can affect latency/throughput/cost-relevant behavior for production inference:
- Serving runtime changes (prefill/decode scheduling, KV cache management, batching/backpressure, memory behavior).
- Kernel changes and kernel auto-selection changes.
- Model serving configuration defaults that affect runtime behavior (e.g., quantization profile defaults, attention/kernels toggles).
- Infrastructure and deployment changes that impact perf measurement (driver/CUDA/runtime/container base images, node configuration).

### Out of scope
- Application-level changes outside Redwood control (customer client code).
- One-off research experiments without a run manifest and reproducible harness.

---

## Canonical metrics and measurement boundaries
Metrics and definitions are standardized per **ADR-0172**. Any new metric must be proposed via an ADR update.

### Primary metrics (must-report)
1) **TTFT (Time To First Token)**
- Definition: time from server request acceptance (server-side) to first token emitted by the server streamer.
- Report: p50, p95, p99.
- Notes: TTFT explicitly excludes client receive/render overhead. Client-side measurements must be labeled as such and cannot be used for gating.

2) **TPOT (Time Per Output Token)**
- Definition: average per-token time during decode, measured server-side from first token emission to last token emission.
- Report: p50, p95, p99.

3) **Throughput**
- For generation/chat: tokens/s (server-side) and optionally req/s (if output length is fixed/controlled).
- For embeddings: req/s and embeddings/s (if batch mode is used).
- Report: mean and p95 where applicable.

### Secondary metrics (required when available)
- **Queue wait time** (scheduler): p50/p95/p99.
- **Batch assembly time**: p50/p95/p99.
- **Batch size distribution**: histogram summary (p50/p95 max).
- **GPU memory peak**: max (MiB) per run.
- **Error rate**: fraction of requests failing (timeout, OOM, internal errors).

### Cold vs warm cache requirements
Because caching materially affects cost/latency and is production-relevant, results must be reported as:
- **Cold**: prefix/KV caches cleared for the workload cohort.
- **Warm**: a defined warmup phase and cache retention enabled.

For gating decisions:
- Default gating uses **Cold** unless a workload is explicitly designated “cache-dependent” in the representative suite spec.
- Warm results are recorded and monitored; large cold↔warm deltas are treated as a diagnostic signal (not a failure by default).

---

## Noise model (how we distinguish regression vs. variance)
Performance data has unavoidable variability (GPU clocks, placement, thermal behavior, background IO, kernel selection changes, cache state). This policy uses a two-level model:

### Level 1: Run-level repeatability
A “run” is a single harness execution with a pinned workload definition + seed + manifest.
- Each gated comparison must include **multiple independent runs** (see minimum sample sizes below).
- Runs must include a standardized warmup period and must capture environment details in the run manifest (driver/CUDA/runtime build, kernel selection mode, scheduler policy, node type, region).

### Level 2: Statistical comparison across runs
We compare a candidate (PR branch / release build) against a baseline (golden build tag for a given suite version).

**Default test statistic:** percent change of the run-median metric across repeats.
- For each metric M, compute the median value per run.
- Across runs, compute the median-of-medians for baseline and candidate.
- Compute %Δ = (candidate - baseline) / baseline.

**Confidence / robustness:**
- Use bootstrap resampling across runs to estimate a 95% confidence interval (CI) on %Δ.
- A change is considered “actionable” only if it clears the **noise floor** and breaches warning/fail thresholds with CI support.

**Noise floor (ignore region):**
- If |%Δ| < **2%** for a metric, treat as noise by default (unless the absolute delta is operationally meaningful; see “absolute thresholds”).

**Absolute thresholds (override noise floor):**
- TTFT: **+20 ms** at any percentile is considered actionable even if <2%.
- TPOT: **+0.20 ms/token** actionable.
- Error rate: **+0.10% absolute** actionable.

**Known high-variance environments:**
- If a particular SKU/region cohort is flagged as high-variance (SRE-owned designation), gating requires either (a) more repeats or (b) an isolated fleet window. High-variance designations must be documented and time-bounded.

---

## Minimum sample sizes (gating requirements)
Minimums are chosen to balance cost with statistical power for typical Redwood workloads.

### A. Minimum repeats (independent runs)
- **PR gating (fast path):** 2 runs (same environment), only for “tier-1 smoke” workloads.
- **Nightly canary:** 3 runs.
- **Release branch gate:** 5 runs (or 3 runs across 2 separate fleet windows).

### B. Minimum requests per run (by workload type)
The representative suite defines workload cohorts; use the cohort type to select minimums:

| Workload cohort | Example | Minimum requests/run | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Chat streaming (short prompts, variable decode) | interactive chat | 10,000 | Must include concurrency ramp and steady-state window. |
| Tool/function calling (bursty multi-turn) | burst + idle cycles | 6,000 | Must include burst period long enough to show queueing dynamics. |
| Long-context prefill stress (large prefill, modest decode) | summarization | 1,200 | Prefill dominates; ensure long-context bin coverage. |
| Embeddings high-QPS (small payloads) | embeddings API | 50,000 | Prefer fixed-size payload bins to reduce confounding. |

### C. Required steady-state criteria (per run)
A run only counts if:
- Warmup is completed (standard warmup defined by the suite).
- At least **10 minutes** of steady-state measurements are collected (embeddings may use 5 minutes if QPS is high and variance is stable).
- Error rate is below the maximum allowed (see thresholds). If errors exceed allowed rate, the run is invalid unless the error itself is the regression under investigation.

---

## Pass / warn / fail thresholds
Thresholds are expressed as % change vs. baseline (negative throughput change is regression). Unless otherwise noted, thresholds apply to **cold-cache** results.

### A. Default thresholds (company-wide)
**Latency metrics (TTFT, TPOT):**

| Metric | Warn if regression ≥ | Fail if regression ≥ |
|---|---:|---:|
| TTFT p50 | +5% | +8% |
| TTFT p95 | +8% | +12% |
| TTFT p99 | +10% | +15% |
| TPOT p50 | +4% | +7% |
| TPOT p95 | +6% | +10% |
| TPOT p99 | +8% | +12% |

**Throughput metrics:**

| Metric | Warn if regression ≥ | Fail if regression ≥ |
|---|---:|---:|
| tokens/s (generation) | -5% | -8% |
| req/s (embeddings) | -6% | -10% |

**Reliability guardrails (hard fail):**
- Error rate increase of **+0.10% absolute** or more (e.g., 0.05% → 0.15%) is a **Fail**.
- Any new OOM pattern that did not occur in baseline is a **Fail** (even if latency improves).

### B. CI-aware gating interpretation
A candidate fails gating when **both** are true:
1) The bootstrap 95% CI of %Δ overlaps the fail region (i.e., regression is statistically supported), and
2) The regression clears the noise floor (or crosses an absolute threshold).

If the point estimate is in fail but CI is wide (insufficient power), the result is “Inconclusive” and must be retried with additional repeats or an isolated fleet window.

### C. Workload-specific overrides
The representative suite may specify tighter or looser thresholds per cohort if justified by:
- Customer impact (SLO sensitivity of that cohort).
- Historical variance characteristics.
- Known scheduling/kernels sensitivity.

Overrides must be documented in the suite definition and reviewed by Serving Runtime + SRE + Observability.

---

## Gating tiers and where policy applies
### Tier 0: Developer local (non-gated)
- Purpose: quick iteration.
- Not authoritative; no formal pass/fail.
- Must still emit a run manifest when shared.

### Tier 1: PR perf checks (gated when enabled)
- Runs a reduced workload subset (“tier-1 smoke”), typically on one region + one SKU.
- Goal: catch large regressions quickly.
- Thresholds: use **Fail** thresholds only (warnings are surfaced but do not block merges unless configured by the owning team).
- If PR gating is flaky, SRE/CI may temporarily disable with an incident/issue and an ETA to restore.

### Tier 2: Nightly perf-canary (always-on)
- Runs the representative suite nightly for supported baselines.
- Produces trend charts and variance panels.
- Failures page the owning triage rotation only if configured as “page-worthy” (see Alerting section).

### Tier 3: Release-branch perf gate (blocking)
Required before a serving runtime release is approved:
- Representative suite green under this policy for the release candidate.
- Runs must include the release’s target regions (minimum: us-east, eu-west) and key SKUs for that release.
- Any waiver must follow the exception process below.

---

## Required artifacts (to make results attributable)
All gated perf results must include:
- **Run manifest** (per ADR-0172) including:
  - Runtime build identifiers (git SHA, build ID), container image digest.
  - Region/SKU/node type, driver/CUDA, and relevant runtime flags.
  - Scheduler policy name + config hash.
  - Kernel selection mode + any pinned kernels.
  - Workload suite version + cohort ID + seed.
- **Results export** containing:
  - Metric definitions, aggregation method, percentile calculation method.
  - Raw summary stats per run (not only deltas).
- **Links** to dashboards/traces:
  - Dashboard panel link (TTFT/TPOT/throughput) for the run.
  - Trace/profiling artifacts when a regression is flagged (see profiling playbook).

Any result without a manifest is not eligible for gating decisions.

---

## Alerting and escalation
### What pages vs. what posts to triage
- **Page (on-call):** release-branch gate failures for tier-1 models/workloads; error-rate/OOM regressions.
- **Triage channel only:** warning-level regressions, inconclusive results, or failures in non-tier-1 cohorts.

Routing is managed by SRE in collaboration with runtime owners.

### Ownership on regression
- If the regression is clearly attributable to a PR: the PR author/owning team is responsible for first response.
- If attribution is unclear: Serving Runtime on-call (or designated triage rotation) coordinates bisect.

---

## Exception (waiver) process
Waivers exist to unblock releases when the business impact of delaying exceeds the perf risk, but they must be rare and time-bounded.

A waiver requires:
1) A written justification including affected cohorts/metrics, expected customer impact, and mitigation.
2) A link to the failing run(s) and manifests.
3) Approval from:
   - Serving Runtime Engineering Lead (or delegate), and
   - SRE Integration Lead (or delegate).

Waivers must include:
- Expiration date (default: 14 days).
- A follow-up issue/ticket to remove the waiver.

---

## Triage checklist (standard playbook)
When a regression is detected:
1) **Validate reproducibility**
   - Rerun with the same manifest (same suite version, seed, and environment) if possible.
   - Confirm warmup was applied and steady-state criteria were met.

2) **Check environment drift**
   - Driver/CUDA/runtime container changes.
   - Node type differences, GPU clock/power settings.
   - Placement/isolation differences.

3) **Attribute to scheduler vs. kernels vs. workload**
   - Inspect queue wait time and batch assembly metrics.
   - Compare batch size distributions.
   - Check kernel selection debug dumps (selected kernel, fallback reasons).

4) **Inspect tail behavior**
   - Identify whether regression is p99-only (queueing contention, cache thrash) vs. broad (kernel/runtime change).
   - Use profiling playbook to collect traces for a small reproduction.

5) **Decide action**
   - Fix forward (preferred) or revert.
   - If neither is immediately possible, pursue a temporary mitigation (pin kernel, adjust backpressure guardrails) with an explicit rollback plan.

---

## Change management
- Proposed changes to thresholds, sample sizes, or metric definitions require review by Observability + SRE + Serving Runtime.
- Any change must document:
  - Rationale (variance data, customer/SLO sensitivity).
  - Expected impact on flake rate and compute cost.
  - Backwards compatibility for dashboard queries and historical comparisons.

---

## Appendix: Practical guidance
### Recommended interpretation
- TTFT p99 regressions often indicate scheduler backpressure, queue discipline changes, or prefill/decode overlap issues.
- TPOT regressions often indicate kernel selection changes, KV cache behavior changes, or decode batch inefficiencies.
- Throughput regressions without latency regressions can indicate conservative batching caps or increased per-request overhead.

### Contact / channels
- #eng-runtime (coordination)
- #eng-sre (fleet stability, variance)
- #eng-platform (dashboards)

(If these channel names change, update this page; do not create duplicate policy pages.)

---

## Noise model (how we distinguish regression vs. variance)
Performance data has unavoidable variability (GPU clocks, placement, thermal behavior, background IO, kernel selection changes, cache state). This policy uses a two-level model:

### Level 1: Run-level repeatability
A “run” is a single harness execution with a pinned workload definition + seed + manifest.
- Each gated comparison must include **multiple independent runs** (see minimum sample sizes below).
- Runs must include a standardized warmup period and must capture environment details in the run manifest (driver/CUDA/runtime build, kernel selection mode, scheduler policy, node type, region).

### Level 2: Statistical comparison across runs
We compare a candidate (PR branch / release build) against a baseline (golden build tag for a given suite version).

**Default test statistic:** percent change of the run-median metric across repeats.
- For each metric M, compute the median value per run.
- Across runs, compute the median-of-medians for baseline and candidate.
- Compute %Δ = (candidate - baseline) / baseline.

**Confidence / robustness:**
- Use bootstrap resampling across runs to estimate a 95% confidence interval (CI) on %Δ.
- A change is considered “actionable” only if it clears the **noise floor** and breaches warning/fail thresholds with CI support.

**Noise floor (ignore region):**
- If |%Δ| < **2%** for a metric, treat as noise by default (unless the absolute delta is operationally meaningful; see “absolute thresholds”).

**Absolute thresholds (override noise floor):**
- TTFT: **+20 ms** at any percentile is considered actionable even if <2%.
- TPOT: **+0.20 ms/token** actionable.
- Error rate: **+0.10% absolute** actionable.

**Known high-variance environments:**
- If a particular SKU/region cohort is flagged as high-variance (SRE-owned designation), gating requires either (a) more repeats or (b) an isolated fleet window. High-variance designations must be documented and time-bounded.

---

## Minimum sample sizes (gating requirements)
Minimums are chosen to balance cost with statistical power for typical Redwood workloads.

### A. Minimum repeats (independent runs)
- **PR gating (fast path):** 2 runs (same environment), only for “tier-1 smoke” workloads.
- **Nightly canary:** 3 runs.
- **Release branch gate:** 5 runs (or 3 runs across 2 separate fleet windows).

### B. Minimum requests per run (by workload type)
The representative suite defines workload cohorts; use the cohort type to select minimums:

| Workload cohort | Example | Minimum requests/run | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Chat streaming (short prompts, variable decode) | interactive chat | 10,000 | Must include concurrency ramp and steady-state window. |
| Tool/function calling (bursty multi-turn) | burst + idle cycles | 6,000 | Must include burst period long enough to show queueing dynamics. |
| Long-context prefill stress (large prefill, modest decode) | summarization | 1,200 | Prefill dominates; ensure long-context bin coverage. |
| Embeddings high-QPS (small payloads) | embeddings API | 50,000 | Prefer fixed-size payload bins to reduce confounding. |

### C. Required steady-state criteria (per run)
A run only counts if:
- Warmup is completed (standard warmup defined by the suite).
- At least **10 minutes** of steady-state measurements are collected (embeddings may use 5 minutes if QPS is high and variance is stable).
- Error rate is below the maximum allowed (see thresholds). If errors exceed allowed rate, the run is invalid unless the error itself is the regression under investigation.

---

## Pass / warn / fail thresholds
Thresholds are expressed as % change vs. baseline (negative throughput change is regression). Unless otherwise noted, thresholds apply to **cold-cache** results.

### A. Default thresholds (company-wide)
**Latency metrics (TTFT, TPOT):**

| Metric | Warn if regression ≥ | Fail if regression ≥ |
|---|---:|---:|
| TTFT p50 | +5% | +8% |
| TTFT p95 | +8% | +12% |
| TTFT p99 | +10% | +15% |
| TPOT p50 | +4% | +7% |
| TPOT p95 | +6% | +10% |
| TPOT p99 | +8% | +12% |

**Throughput metrics:**

| Metric | Warn if regression ≥ | Fail if regression ≥ |
|---|---:|---:|
| tokens/s (generation) | -5% | -8% |
| req/s (embeddings) | -6% | -10% |

**Reliability guardrails (hard fail):**
- Error rate increase of **+0.10% absolute** or more (e.g., 0.05% → 0.15%) is a **Fail**.
- Any new OOM pattern that did not occur in baseline is a **Fail** (even if latency improves).

### B. CI-aware gating interpretation
A candidate fails gating when **both** are true:
1) The bootstrap 95% CI of %Δ overlaps the fail region (i.e., regression is statistically supported), and
2) The regression clears the noise floor (or crosses an absolute threshold).

If the point estimate is in fail but CI is wide (insufficient power), the result is “Inconclusive” and must be retried with additional repeats or an isolated fleet window.

### C. Workload-specific overrides
The representative suite may specify tighter or looser thresholds per cohort if justified by:
- Customer impact (SLO sensitivity of that cohort).
- Historical variance characteristics.
- Known scheduling/kernels sensitivity.

Overrides must be documented in the suite definition and reviewed by Serving Runtime + SRE + Observability.

---

## Gating tiers and where policy applies
### Tier 0: Developer local (non-gated)
- Purpose: quick iteration.
- Not authoritative; no formal pass/fail.
- Must still emit a run manifest when shared.

### Tier 1: PR perf checks (gated when enabled)
- Runs a reduced workload subset (“tier-1 smoke”), typically on one region + one SKU.
- Goal: catch large regressions quickly.
- Thresholds: use **Fail** thresholds only (warnings are surfaced but do not block merges unless configured by the owning team).
- If PR gating is flaky, SRE/CI may temporarily disable with an incident/issue and an ETA to restore.

### Tier 2: Nightly perf-canary (always-on)
- Runs the representative suite nightly for supported baselines.
- Produces trend charts and variance panels.
- Failures page the owning triage rotation only if configured as “page-worthy” (see Alerting section).

### Tier 3: Release-branch perf gate (blocking)
Required before a serving runtime release is approved:
- Representative suite green under this policy for the release candidate.
- Runs must include the release’s target regions (minimum: us-east, eu-west) and key SKUs for that release.
- Any waiver must follow the exception process below.

---

## Required artifacts (to make results attributable)
All gated perf results must include:
- **Run manifest** (per ADR-0172) including:
  - Runtime build identifiers (git SHA, build ID), container image digest.
  - Region/SKU/node type, driver/CUDA, and relevant runtime flags.
  - Scheduler policy name + config hash.
  - Kernel selection mode + any pinned kernels.
  - Workload suite version + cohort ID + seed.
- **Results export** containing:
  - Metric definitions, aggregation method, percentile calculation method.
  - Raw summary stats per run (not only deltas).
- **Links** to dashboards/traces:
  - Dashboard panel link (TTFT/TPOT/throughput) for the run.
  - Trace/profiling artifacts when a regression is flagged (see profiling playbook).

Any result without a manifest is not eligible for gating decisions.

---

## Alerting and escalation
### What pages vs. what posts to triage
- **Page (on-call):** release-branch gate failures for tier-1 models/workloads; error-rate/OOM regressions.
- **Triage channel only:** warning-level regressions, inconclusive results, or failures in non-tier-1 cohorts.

Routing is managed by SRE in collaboration with runtime owners.

### Ownership on regression
- If the regression is clearly attributable to a PR: the PR author/owning team is responsible for first response.
- If attribution is unclear: Serving Runtime on-call (or designated triage rotation) coordinates bisect.

---

## Exception (waiver) process
Waivers exist to unblock releases when the business impact of delaying exceeds the perf risk, but they must be rare and time-bounded.

A waiver requires:
1) A written justification including affected cohorts/metrics, expected customer impact, and mitigation.
2) A link to the failing run(s) and manifests.
3) Approval from:
   - Serving Runtime Engineering Lead (or delegate), and
   - SRE Integration Lead (or delegate).

Waivers must include:
- Expiration date (default: 14 days).
- A follow-up issue/ticket to remove the waiver.

---

## Triage checklist (standard playbook)
When a regression is detected:
1) **Validate reproducibility**
   - Rerun with the same manifest (same suite version, seed, and environment) if possible.
   - Confirm warmup was applied and steady-state criteria were met.

2) **Check environment drift**
   - Driver/CUDA/runtime container changes.
   - Node type differences, GPU clock/power settings.
   - Placement/isolation differences.

3) **Attribute to scheduler vs. kernels vs. workload**
   - Inspect queue wait time and batch assembly metrics.
   - Compare batch size distributions.
   - Check kernel selection debug dumps (selected kernel, fallback reasons).

4) **Inspect tail behavior**
   - Identify whether regression is p99-only (queueing contention, cache thrash) vs. broad (kernel/runtime change).
   - Use profiling playbook to collect traces for a small reproduction.

5) **Decide action**
   - Fix forward (preferred) or revert.
   - If neither is immediately possible, pursue a temporary mitigation (pin kernel, adjust backpressure guardrails) with an explicit rollback plan.

---

## Change management
- Proposed changes to thresholds, sample sizes, or metric definitions require review by Observability + SRE + Serving Runtime.
- Any change must document:
  - Rationale (variance data, customer/SLO sensitivity).
  - Expected impact on flake rate and compute cost.
  - Backwards compatibility for dashboard queries and historical comparisons.

---

## Appendix: Practical guidance
### Recommended interpretation
- TTFT p99 regressions often indicate scheduler backpressure, queue discipline changes, or prefill/decode overlap issues.
- TPOT regressions often indicate kernel selection changes, KV cache behavior changes, or decode batch inefficiencies.
- Throughput regressions without latency regressions can indicate conservative batching caps or increased per-request overhead.

### Contact / channels
- #eng-runtime (coordination)
- #eng-sre (fleet stability, variance)
- #eng-platform (dashboards)

(If these channel names change, update this page; do not create duplicate policy pages.)

source.title: ADR-023: Workload-aware batching defaults via three SLO tiers (fast / standard / cost-efficient)
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.content:
## Status
**Accepted** (published)

## Decision
We will ship **three customer-facing batching default profiles** aligned to **latency SLO tiers**:
- **fast** (tail-latency protection)
- **standard** (balanced default)
- **cost-efficient** (throughput/$ optimization)

Defaults will be **workload-aware within the tier** (using a small set of runtime signals such as prompt-length buckets, streaming vs non-streaming, and observed concurrency/queue pressure), but we will **not** ship **per-tenant auto-tuned batching** in the initial release.

We will provide a clear **override hierarchy** and **reason codes** so operators can understand and control effective behavior:
1) Customer override (Dedicated/Enterprise) 
2) Route/model policy selection (control plane)
3) Tier default (fast/standard/cost-efficient)
4) Safety fallback / overload mode constraints (runtime)

## Context
Redwood’s inference runtime uses continuous batching and queueing to improve throughput and cost. Historically, many performance issues and on-call incidents have been driven by:
- Customers selecting unsafe batching knobs (oversized batches, long wait budgets)
- Mixed workloads (short prompts + long prompts; streaming + non-streaming) causing fairness/starvation edge cases
- Unclear trade-offs between latency and cost, and unclear responsibility boundaries (customer vs platform)

We want to make batching safer and more automatic while preserving predictable tail latency. The product requirement is a customer-visible set of defaults that map to latency expectations and can be rolled out safely across Hosted API and Dedicated.

A key architecture choice is whether to:
- (A) implement **per-tenant auto-tuning** (continuously finding optimal batching knobs per tenant/workload), or
- (B) ship a small number of **tiered defaults** with workload-aware heuristics and strong observability.

## Drivers
### Product & customer experience
- Provide **simple, explainable choices** that map to customer intent ("I need low latency" vs "I want lower cost")
- Keep a consistent interface across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private
- Reduce the need for customers to become batching experts

### Reliability & operations
- Preserve tail latency SLOs (p95/p99) and avoid starvation under mixed workloads
- Ensure predictable behavior under overload (backpressure/load shedding) with rapid rollback
- Provide monitoring and reason codes that support debugging and customer support

### Engineering velocity
- Avoid a multi-quarter effort to build safe online tuning loops, guardrails, and continuous per-tenant experimentation
- Minimize blast radius from incorrect tuning decisions

## Considered options
### Option 1: Per-tenant online auto-tuning (rejected for initial release)
**Description:** The system continuously explores/optimizes batching knobs per tenant (or per route) based on live traffic, cost, and latency.

**Pros**
- Potentially highest efficiency per workload
- Tailored to each tenant’s prompt distributions and concurrency patterns

**Cons**
- Harder to guarantee SLOs during exploration; risk of negative latency or correctness regressions
- Complex governance: when tuning is "allowed", how to roll back, how to explain outcomes to customers
- Requires stronger experimentation platform and statistical decisioning to avoid flapping
- High operational burden (many unique configurations) and challenging incident triage
- Risk of regressions tied to small traffic shifts (non-stationary workloads)

**Conclusion:** Valuable long-term, but too risky and complex for the initial launch given reliability constraints and the desire for explainability.

### Option 2: Static global defaults (rejected)
**Description:** One set of default knobs per model family/hardware class, not workload-aware.

**Pros**
- Simple to implement and validate

**Cons**
- Performs poorly on mixed workloads and streaming
- Doesn’t address the core requirement: workload-aware safer defaults

**Conclusion:** Insufficient to meet goals.

### Option 3: Three SLO-tier defaults + workload-aware heuristics (accepted)
**Description:** Publish three tiers with explicit SLO intent and ship runtime heuristics that adjust within tier using a small set of workload signals.

**Pros**
- Clear customer choices; aligns to routing/policy controls and product messaging
- Easier to validate with benchmark suites and canary gates
- Lower config cardinality; better operational predictability
- Enables safe incremental rollout with kill switches

**Cons**
- Not perfectly optimal for every tenant
- Requires careful design to prevent tier mis-selection and to avoid hidden behavior changes

**Conclusion:** Best balance of value, safety, and time-to-ship.

## Decision details
### Tier semantics (high-level)
- **fast:** Prioritize tail latency and interactive responsiveness; tighter queue wait budgets; stricter admission control; stronger fairness protections.
- **standard:** Balanced defaults for typical production traffic; moderate batching; stable streaming.
- **cost-efficient:** Maximize throughput and reduce $/1K tokens; more aggressive batching; additional safeguards against queue blowups.

Notes:
- Numeric tier targets are defined and owned by SRE in the SLO tier definition page (linked above). This ADR intentionally does not freeze numbers.
- Tier selection is exposed at the **route/model policy** level with SDK support where applicable.

### Workload-aware signals (initial set)
The scheduler may adapt within a tier using limited, bounded-cardinality signals:
- Prompt length bucket (e.g., small/medium/large)
- Streaming vs non-streaming
- Observed concurrency / queue depth band

Signals must be:
- explainable (used to generate reason codes)
- bounded in cardinality (avoid telemetry cost blowups)
- safe to compute without customer data retention changes

### Overrides and precedence
- Dedicated and enterprise customers may configure explicit overrides (within allowed guardrails).
- Overload mode behavior remains a runtime safety mechanism and may restrict batching even if a customer requested a more aggressive tier.

### Observability requirements
We will emit:
- Effective tier/profile (including version)
- Reason codes for selection path (e.g., tier default, customer override, model constraint, overload mode)
- Per-tier latency histograms and queueing metrics (with cardinality controls)

## Consequences
### Positive
- Customers get a safe default with a clear intent-to-outcome mapping.
- Redwood can publish benchmarks and set expectations without overfitting to any one tenant.
- Incident response improves: fewer unique configurations and explicit reason codes.

### Negative / trade-offs
- Some tenants will be under-optimized versus what a bespoke tuned configuration could achieve.
- There is a risk that customers interpret tiers as guaranteed outcomes across all workloads. Messaging must be careful ("targets" and "typical" conditions).

## Risks and mitigations
### Risk 1: Tail latency regressions (especially fast tier)
**Failure mode:** Short interactive requests are delayed behind long prefill workloads; p99 spikes; streaming time-to-first-token (TTFT) degrades.

**Mitigations**
- Fast-tier wait budget caps and admission control
- Anti-starvation fairness policies for mixed prompt lengths
- Canary gates: block rollout if fast-tier p99 regresses beyond thresholds for top model families/regions
- Explicit overload mode that prefers shedding load vs silently increasing queue times

### Risk 2: Queue blowups / instability in cost-efficient tier
**Failure mode:** Aggressive batching increases queue depth; customers see unpredictable latency and timeouts during bursts.

**Mitigations**
- Hard caps on max batch tokens and max wait time
- Backpressure mechanisms and load shedding thresholds
- Separate dashboards/alerts for queue depth and timeout rates with tier breakdown

### Risk 3: Metric cardinality explosion
**Failure mode:** Adding tier/profile dimensions increases telemetry costs or breaks dashboards.

**Mitigations**
- Tier labels limited to {fast, standard, cost-efficient}
- Profile versioning as low-cardinality (e.g., integer or short semantic version), not arbitrary strings per request
- Sampling and aggregation rules documented in metrics taxonomy

### Risk 4: Customer confusion and support burden
**Failure mode:** Customers don’t know which tier to choose; assume fast tier is always best; misconfigure Dedicated overrides.

**Mitigations**
- Console surfacing: recommended tier + "why this profile" explanation
- Support playbook and public docs FAQ (defaults, overrides, troubleshooting)
- Clear policy precedence and validation errors for invalid combinations

### Risk 5: Quality/correctness regressions attributed to batching
**Failure mode:** Changes in scheduling correlate with output differences (e.g., truncation edge cases, streaming chunking behavior).

**Mitigations**
- Quality guardrails and targeted evals for batching/quantization interactions
- Regression alerts tied to prompt sets and golden outputs
- Rollback plan with immediate kill switch

## Rollout plan (architecture commitments)
We will support staged enablement with an explicit kill switch:
1) Internal canary (Redwood-owned traffic)
2) Hosted API subset (feature-flagged by route/model)
3) Dedicated opt-in (per tenant/cluster)
4) Gradual broader default

Each stage requires:
- Perf canary gates passing
- Error budget impact within agreed bounds
- Dashboards and alerts live with on-call routing
- Support/docs readiness

## Non-goals (explicit)
- Continuous per-tenant auto-tuning with exploration/exploitation in this release.
- Cross-region routing changes beyond existing smart routing policies.

## Follow-ups
- Publish tier-specific recommended profiles per major model family and hardware class.
- Implement reason code taxonomy and ensure it is stable for support automation.
- Add a review checkpoint after broad rollout to assess whether additional tiers or limited auto-tuning would provide meaningful incremental value.

## Open questions
- Final numeric SLO targets per tier and how to phrase them externally (owned by SRE + Product).
- Whether tier selection should be allowed per-request for Hosted API beyond a controlled header/SDK parameter (risk: abuse and unpredictable capacity planning).
- How to represent tier/profile changes in customer-facing change logs and Console audit history.

## Appendix: Decision owners
- Executive sponsor: Mateo Alvarez
- Engineering lead (Scheduling): Sofia Ivanova
- Runtime architecture review: Noah Patel
- SRE review: Sean Gallagher
- Product review: Aditya Rao, Nikhil Sharma

text:
## Status
**Accepted** (published)

## Decision
We will ship **three customer-facing batching default profiles** aligned to **latency SLO tiers**:
- **fast** (tail-latency protection)
- **standard** (balanced default)
- **cost-efficient** (throughput/$ optimization)

Defaults will be **workload-aware within the tier** (using a small set of runtime signals such as prompt-length buckets, streaming vs non-streaming, and observed concurrency/queue pressure), but we will **not** ship **per-tenant auto-tuned batching** in the initial release.

We will provide a clear **override hierarchy** and **reason codes** so operators can understand and control effective behavior:
1) Customer override (Dedicated/Enterprise) 
2) Route/model policy selection (control plane)
3) Tier default (fast/standard/cost-efficient)
4) Safety fallback / overload mode constraints (runtime)

## Context
Redwood’s inference runtime uses continuous batching and queueing to improve throughput and cost. Historically, many performance issues and on-call incidents have been driven by:
- Customers selecting unsafe batching knobs (oversized batches, long wait budgets)
- Mixed workloads (short prompts + long prompts; streaming + non-streaming) causing fairness/starvation edge cases
- Unclear trade-offs between latency and cost, and unclear responsibility boundaries (customer vs platform)

We want to make batching safer and more automatic while preserving predictable tail latency. The product requirement is a customer-visible set of defaults that map to latency expectations and can be rolled out safely across Hosted API and Dedicated.

A key architecture choice is whether to:
- (A) implement **per-tenant auto-tuning** (continuously finding optimal batching knobs per tenant/workload), or
- (B) ship a small number of **tiered defaults** with workload-aware heuristics and strong observability.

## Drivers
### Product & customer experience
- Provide **simple, explainable choices** that map to customer intent ("I need low latency" vs "I want lower cost")
- Keep a consistent interface across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private
- Reduce the need for customers to become batching experts

### Reliability & operations
- Preserve tail latency SLOs (p95/p99) and avoid starvation under mixed workloads
- Ensure predictable behavior under overload (backpressure/load shedding) with rapid rollback
- Provide monitoring and reason codes that support debugging and customer support

### Engineering velocity
- Avoid a multi-quarter effort to build safe online tuning loops, guardrails, and continuous per-tenant experimentation
- Minimize blast radius from incorrect tuning decisions

## Considered options
### Option 1: Per-tenant online auto-tuning (rejected for initial release)
**Description:** The system continuously explores/optimizes batching knobs per tenant (or per route) based on live traffic, cost, and latency.

**Pros**
- Potentially highest efficiency per workload
- Tailored to each tenant’s prompt distributions and concurrency patterns

**Cons**
- Harder to guarantee SLOs during exploration; risk of negative latency or correctness regressions
- Complex governance: when tuning is "allowed", how to roll back, how to explain outcomes to customers
- Requires stronger experimentation platform and statistical decisioning to avoid flapping
- High operational burden (many unique configurations) and challenging incident triage
- Risk of regressions tied to small traffic shifts (non-stationary workloads)

**Conclusion:** Valuable long-term, but too risky and complex for the initial launch given reliability constraints and the desire for explainability.

### Option 2: Static global defaults (rejected)
**Description:** One set of default knobs per model family/hardware class, not workload-aware.

**Pros**
- Simple to implement and validate

**Cons**
- Performs poorly on mixed workloads and streaming
- Doesn’t address the core requirement: workload-aware safer defaults

**Conclusion:** Insufficient to meet goals.

### Option 3: Three SLO-tier defaults + workload-aware heuristics (accepted)
**Description:** Publish three tiers with explicit SLO intent and ship runtime heuristics that adjust within tier using a small set of workload signals.

**Pros**
- Clear customer choices; aligns to routing/policy controls and product messaging
- Easier to validate with benchmark suites and canary gates
- Lower config cardinality; better operational predictability
- Enables safe incremental rollout with kill switches

**Cons**
- Not perfectly optimal for every tenant
- Requires careful design to prevent tier mis-selection and to avoid hidden behavior changes

**Conclusion:** Best balance of value, safety, and time-to-ship.

## Decision details
### Tier semantics (high-level)
- **fast:** Prioritize tail latency and interactive responsiveness; tighter queue wait budgets; stricter admission control; stronger fairness protections.
- **standard:** Balanced defaults for typical production traffic; moderate batching; stable streaming.
- **cost-efficient:** Maximize throughput and reduce $/1K tokens; more aggressive batching; additional safeguards against queue blowups.

Notes:
- Numeric tier targets are defined and owned by SRE in the SLO tier definition page (linked above). This ADR intentionally does not freeze numbers.
- Tier selection is exposed at the **route/model policy** level with SDK support where applicable.

### Workload-aware signals (initial set)
The scheduler may adapt within a tier using limited, bounded-cardinality signals:
- Prompt length bucket (e.g., small/medium/large)
- Streaming vs non-streaming
- Observed concurrency / queue depth band

Signals must be:
- explainable (used to generate reason codes)
- bounded in cardinality (avoid telemetry cost blowups)
- safe to compute without customer data retention changes

### Overrides and precedence
- Dedicated and enterprise customers may configure explicit overrides (within allowed guardrails).
- Overload mode behavior remains a runtime safety mechanism and may restrict batching even if a customer requested a more aggressive tier.

### Observability requirements
We will emit:
- Effective tier/profile (including version)
- Reason codes for selection path (e.g., tier default, customer override, model constraint, overload mode)
- Per-tier latency histograms and queueing metrics (with cardinality controls)

## Consequences
### Positive
- Customers get a safe default with a clear intent-to-outcome mapping.
- Redwood can publish benchmarks and set expectations without overfitting to any one tenant.
- Incident response improves: fewer unique configurations and explicit reason codes.

### Negative / trade-offs
- Some tenants will be under-optimized versus what a bespoke tuned configuration could achieve.
- There is a risk that customers interpret tiers as guaranteed outcomes across all workloads. Messaging must be careful ("targets" and "typical" conditions).

## Risks and mitigations
### Risk 1: Tail latency regressions (especially fast tier)
**Failure mode:** Short interactive requests are delayed behind long prefill workloads; p99 spikes; streaming time-to-first-token (TTFT) degrades.

**Mitigations**
- Fast-tier wait budget caps and admission control
- Anti-starvation fairness policies for mixed prompt lengths
- Canary gates: block rollout if fast-tier p99 regresses beyond thresholds for top model families/regions
- Explicit overload mode that prefers shedding load vs silently increasing queue times

### Risk 2: Queue blowups / instability in cost-efficient tier
**Failure mode:** Aggressive batching increases queue depth; customers see unpredictable latency and timeouts during bursts.

**Mitigations**
- Hard caps on max batch tokens and max wait time
- Backpressure mechanisms and load shedding thresholds
- Separate dashboards/alerts for queue depth and timeout rates with tier breakdown

### Risk 3: Metric cardinality explosion
**Failure mode:** Adding tier/profile dimensions increases telemetry costs or breaks dashboards.

**Mitigations**
- Tier labels limited to {fast, standard, cost-efficient}
- Profile versioning as low-cardinality (e.g., integer or short semantic version), not arbitrary strings per request
- Sampling and aggregation rules documented in metrics taxonomy

### Risk 4: Customer confusion and support burden
**Failure mode:** Customers don’t know which tier to choose; assume fast tier is always best; misconfigure Dedicated overrides.

**Mitigations**
- Console surfacing: recommended tier + "why this profile" explanation
- Support playbook and public docs FAQ (defaults, overrides, troubleshooting)
- Clear policy precedence and validation errors for invalid combinations

### Risk 5: Quality/correctness regressions attributed to batching
**Failure mode:** Changes in scheduling correlate with output differences (e.g., truncation edge cases, streaming chunking behavior).

**Mitigations**
- Quality guardrails and targeted evals for batching/quantization interactions
- Regression alerts tied to prompt sets and golden outputs
- Rollback plan with immediate kill switch

## Rollout plan (architecture commitments)
We will support staged enablement with an explicit kill switch:
1) Internal canary (Redwood-owned traffic)
2) Hosted API subset (feature-flagged by route/model)
3) Dedicated opt-in (per tenant/cluster)
4) Gradual broader default

Each stage requires:
- Perf canary gates passing
- Error budget impact within agreed bounds
- Dashboards and alerts live with on-call routing
- Support/docs readiness

## Non-goals (explicit)
- Continuous per-tenant auto-tuning with exploration/exploitation in this release.
- Cross-region routing changes beyond existing smart routing policies.

## Follow-ups
- Publish tier-specific recommended profiles per major model family and hardware class.
- Implement reason code taxonomy and ensure it is stable for support automation.
- Add a review checkpoint after broad rollout to assess whether additional tiers or limited auto-tuning would provide meaningful incremental value.

## Open questions
- Final numeric SLO targets per tier and how to phrase them externally (owned by SRE + Product).
- Whether tier selection should be allowed per-request for Hosted API beyond a controlled header/SDK parameter (risk: abuse and unpredictable capacity planning).
- How to represent tier/profile changes in customer-facing change logs and Console audit history.

## Appendix: Decision owners
- Executive sponsor: Mateo Alvarez
- Engineering lead (Scheduling): Sofia Ivanova
- Runtime architecture review: Noah Patel
- SRE review: Sean Gallagher
- Product review: Aditya Rao, Nikhil Sharma

source.title: Contract Gates and Canary Mesh Guidelines for Service APIs (2026)
source.author: Miguel Alvarez
source.body:
Summary:
This document defines the contract validation, runtime gating, and canary mesh requirements for Redwood service APIs. It prescribes a single reproducible flow from code change to gradual traffic rollout that enforces behavioral compatibility, performance/observability gates, and automatic rollback criteria. Goal: reduce customer-facing regressions caused by API changes while enabling fast, iterative service delivery.

Who this is for:
- Service owners and backend engineers implementing HTTP/gRPC APIs
- SDK and client library maintainers
- Release engineers and SREs configuring rollout pipelines
- QA and automated testing teams responsible for contract tests

Principles:
1) Consumer-driven validation: tests authored from client expectations (integration harnesses, recorded interactions) are authoritative for compatibility checks.
2) Repeatable gates: contract checks must run in CI, pre-merge, and as part of the canary pipeline with identical assertions.
3) Observability-first rollouts: any canary must emit structured telemetry (traces, metrics, logs) aligned with standardized tags.
4) Automated safe-fail: if any gate fails, the system must automatically stop rollout and optionally roll back to the previous stable revision.

Required artifacts and registry:
- OpenAPI/Proto artifact: every public/internal API must publish a canonical artifact (openapi.yaml or service.proto) to the Contract Registry at build time. Filename convention: <service>-<env>-schema-YYYYMMDD.yaml.
- Consumer contract bundle: a zip of consumer-originized tests and sample requests (cdctests.zip). Stored in the Contract Registry with consumer owner metadata.
- Compatibility report: autogenerated JSON (flat) summarizing added/removed endpoints, schema diffs, and test pass/fail — attached to the PR.

Compatibility policy (short):
- Non-breaking changes: additive request fields, optional response fields, new endpoints, backwards-compatible enum additions.
- Breaking changes: removing or renaming required fields, changing response shapes for existing fields, removing endpoints, changing error semantics (status codes) for stable routes.
- Semver mapping: services must increment minor version for additive changes, major for breaking changes. A documented migration plan is required for major changes.

Contract test requirements:
- Mandatory: a consumer-driven contract (CDC) test suite that can be executed in CI with a single CLI command: ./contract-run --service service-name --env staging.
- Test types: schema validation, example-based request/response matching, error-path exercises, authentication/authorization verification.
- Performance smoke: CDC must include a small perf smoke check (50 concurrent requests or load profile representative of the consumer) to catch obvious latency regressions during canary.
- Output: tests must emit machine-readable TAP or JUnit XML and a summary JSON with flat key/values (no nested objects) for CI gates.

CI and build integration (required steps):
1) Build publishes API artifact to Contract Registry.
2) CI runs static schema-diff against latest published artifact; diffs are attached to PR.
3) CI executes CDC suite; failures block merge.
4) If a PR modifies schema and is non-breaking, the author adds a compatibility note; if breaking, the PR must reference a migration plan and be routed through product review.

Canary mesh and runtime gating (operational flow):
- Definitions:
  - Canary: a new deployment revision receiving a controlled percentage of production traffic (typically starting at 0.5%).
  - Mesh: sidecar-level traffic routing used to mirror/shadow traffic and steer percentages.
- Minimum canary sequence (automated):
  1) Deploy canary revision in the same cluster/region as primary.
  2) Run smoke/end-to-end synthetic checks (success criteria defined below).
  3) Shadow 100% of traffic to canary for passive validation (no client impact).
  4) If passive validation passes, shift 0.5% of production traffic to canary for 15 minutes.
  5) Evaluate gates; if pass, double traffic to 1% for 30 minutes, then 5% for 60 minutes, then full (100%) if all gates pass.
  6) If any gate fails, automatically rollback to previous revision and create incident ticket with reproducible failure artifacts.

Gates and thresholds (must be enforced by the rollout controller):
- Correctness gate: all CDC suites must pass (zero failures).
- Error budget gate: error rate increase on canary compared to baseline must be <= 0.5% absolute or <= 10% relative (whichever is larger).
- Latency gate: p95 must not increase more than 15% absolute or exceed 350ms (whichever is smaller).
- Throughput degradation: successful request throughput must be within 5% of baseline under the same load.
- Tracing/Span completeness: new revision must emit the standardized span name format service:<service>:operation and include trace tags: request_id, tenant_id, route, canary_revision. Missing spans or tags are a fail.
- Logging schema gate: structured logs must include fields timestamp, level, request_id, service, revision, error_code (if present). Logs missing these fields for >0.1% of requests trigger fail.

Observability requirements (instrumentation checklist):
- Metrics: request_count, request_success_count, request_error_count, request_latency_ms_bucketed (histogram), kv_cache_hit_rate (if applicable), active_rps.
- Labels/tags: service, region, revision, environment, route, tenant_id (when applicable).
- Traces: distributed traces for all external-facing endpoints, sampling rate >= 5% in production, 100% for canary traffic for the duration of the canary window.
- Dashboards: canonical per-service dashboard must include p50/p95/p99, error rate, request rate, and a canary overlay.

Performance and cost constraints during rollout:
- Short-load test: during the 5% stage, run a 10-minute load test at 1.5x expected canary traffic to validate auto-scaling and memory pressure.
- Cost throttles: if per-minute token cost (for services integrating LLM inference) increases by >20% vs baseline during canary, gate fails and rollback triggers.

Example contract diff and CLI snippet (example YAML/commands):
- Schema diff excerpt (human-readable):
  * Added /v2/users/details (new endpoint)
  * Response: users[].profile (optional object) added field nickname: string (optional)
  * Removed status_code 418 from /v1/health (BREAKING)
- Running contract checks locally:
  # publish artifact locally and run contract tests against staging
  ./tools/publish-contract --file openapi.yaml --registry http://contracts.local:8080
  ./contract-run --service billing --env staging --report output/junit.xml

Rollout automation implementation notes:
- The rollout controller (service-rollout) owns gating enforcement and must accept a flat JSON gate-definition (no nested fields). It calls the canary mesh API to adjust traffic and queries the metrics API for current windows.
- All gate decisions must be auditable and produce an append-only event log in the audit store with keys: timestamp, service, revision, gate_name, value, decision.

Failure modes and playbook (runbook excerpt):
1) Gate fail detected during 0.5% stage: rollback immediately, create incident P1 if error_rate > 1% absolute. Notify service owner and SRE rotation.
2) Missing traces/tags: suspend rollout and open ticket; if instrumentation is unavailable for >30 minutes, rollback.
3) Gradual latency drift within thresholds but increasing trend: hold at current percentage and extend validation window to 2x before proceeding.

Ownership and contacts:
- Policy owner: API Platform (team alias: api-platform@redwood.ai).
- Rollout controller maintainers: release-engineering@redwood.ai.
- Contract Registry operators: infra-contracts@redwood.ai.

How to adopt (migration checklist for existing services):
- Stage 1: Publish canonical artifact and add minimal CDC tests (schema + one success/error sample).
- Stage 2: Integrate contract-run into service CI and block merges on failures.
- Stage 3: Enable passive shadowing in pre-prod and inspect observability artifacts.
- Stage 4: Run a staged canary with a low-traffic route and validate automation.

Governance and exceptions:
- Exceptions for internal-only ephemeral services can be requested via a ticket to api-platform; exceptions must include documented compensating controls.
- Breaking change approvals require sign-off from product, security (if auth/PII changes), and API Platform.

Appendix: Quick reference table (human-readable lines):
- Canary progression: 0.5% (15m) -> 1% (30m) -> 5% (60m) -> 100%
- Key gates: contract pass, error_rate_delta <= 0.5% abs, p95_delta <= 15% abs / p95 <= 350ms, traces present 100% for canary
- CLI entrypoints: contract-run, tools/publish-contract, rolloutctl set-traffic

Related reading and links:
- See API compatibility policy for versioning semantics (related page).
- See Release Automation repo for rollout controller implementation notes.

Revision history:
- 2025-11-12: Initial draft authored by Miguel Alvarez.
- 2026-01-08: Added performance and cost throttles (reviewer: Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-02-03: Finalized gate thresholds and automation contract (published).

Questions or feedback: please file a PR on this page or ping api-platform@redwood.ai.

- Logging schema gate: structured logs must include fields timestamp, level, request_id, service, revision, error_code (if present). Logs missing these fields for >0.1% of requests trigger fail.

Observability requirements (instrumentation checklist):
- Metrics: request_count, request_success_count, request_error_count, request_latency_ms_bucketed (histogram), kv_cache_hit_rate (if applicable), active_rps.
- Labels/tags: service, region, revision, environment, route, tenant_id (when applicable).
- Traces: distributed traces for all external-facing endpoints, sampling rate >= 5% in production, 100% for canary traffic for the duration of the canary window.
- Dashboards: canonical per-service dashboard must include p50/p95/p99, error rate, request rate, and a canary overlay.

Performance and cost constraints during rollout:
- Short-load test: during the 5% stage, run a 10-minute load test at 1.5x expected canary traffic to validate auto-scaling and memory pressure.
- Cost throttles: if per-minute token cost (for services integrating LLM inference) increases by >20% vs baseline during canary, gate fails and rollback triggers.

Example contract diff and CLI snippet (example YAML/commands):
- Schema diff excerpt (human-readable):
  * Added /v2/users/details (new endpoint)
  * Response: users[].profile (optional object) added field nickname: string (optional)
  * Removed status_code 418 from /v1/health (BREAKING)
- Running contract checks locally:
  # publish artifact locally and run contract tests against staging
  ./tools/publish-contract --file openapi.yaml --registry http://contracts.local:8080
  ./contract-run --service billing --env staging --report output/junit.xml

Rollout automation implementation notes:
- The rollout controller (service-rollout) owns gating enforcement and must accept a flat JSON gate-definition (no nested fields). It calls the canary mesh API to adjust traffic and queries the metrics API for current windows.
- All gate decisions must be auditable and produce an append-only event log in the audit store with keys: timestamp, service, revision, gate_name, value, decision.

Failure modes and playbook (runbook excerpt):
1) Gate fail detected during 0.5% stage: rollback immediately, create incident P1 if error_rate > 1% absolute. Notify service owner and SRE rotation.
2) Missing traces/tags: suspend rollout and open ticket; if instrumentation is unavailable for >30 minutes, rollback.
3) Gradual latency drift within thresholds but increasing trend: hold at current percentage and extend validation window to 2x before proceeding.

Ownership and contacts:
- Policy owner: API Platform (team alias: api-platform@redwood.ai).
- Rollout controller maintainers: release-engineering@redwood.ai.
- Contract Registry operators: infra-contracts@redwood.ai.

How to adopt (migration checklist for existing services):
- Stage 1: Publish canonical artifact and add minimal CDC tests (schema + one success/error sample).
- Stage 2: Integrate contract-run into service CI and block merges on failures.
- Stage 3: Enable passive shadowing in pre-prod and inspect observability artifacts.
- Stage 4: Run a staged canary with a low-traffic route and validate automation.

Governance and exceptions:
- Exceptions for internal-only ephemeral services can be requested via a ticket to api-platform; exceptions must include documented compensating controls.
- Breaking change approvals require sign-off from product, security (if auth/PII changes), and API Platform.

Appendix: Quick reference table (human-readable lines):
- Canary progression: 0.5% (15m) -> 1% (30m) -> 5% (60m) -> 100%
- Key gates: contract pass, error_rate_delta <= 0.5% abs, p95_delta <= 15% abs / p95 <= 350ms, traces present 100% for canary
- CLI entrypoints: contract-run, tools/publish-contract, rolloutctl set-traffic

Related reading and links:
- See API compatibility policy for versioning semantics (related page).
- See Release Automation repo for rollout controller implementation notes.

Revision history:
- 2025-11-12: Initial draft authored by Miguel Alvarez.
- 2026-01-08: Added performance and cost throttles (reviewer: Priya Natarajan).
- 2026-02-03: Finalized gate thresholds and automation contract (published).

Questions or feedback: please file a PR on this page or ping api-platform@redwood.ai.

source.title: Progressive Degradation and Quality Fallback Playbook
source.author: Aisha Morgan
source.body:
Overview

This playbook defines an operational pattern—progressive request degradation with quality-aware fallbacks—that lets Redwood keep customer-facing LLM features online during transient capacity, hardware, or downstream-service problems while bounding user-visible quality regressions and unit cost. It documents service-level thresholds, decision logic, implementation knobs, and monitoring / runbook steps.

Goals

- Keep latency-sensitive endpoints responsive under stress with graceful, testable quality reductions.
- Make degradations predictable and auditable: every fallback path must be covered by automated tests, rollout rules, and metrics.
- Optimize for user-perceived utility first, cost second: prefer semantic-preserving fallbacks over blind truncation.
- Provide a single canonical implementation and configuration schema used by hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

Scope

Applies to: Redwood API generation endpoints (including streaming), chat APIs, dedicated model routes, and runtime-sidecar behaviors for Private deployments. Excluded: offline batch embeddings and long-running fine-tune jobs.

When to invoke progressive degradation

1) System-level triggers:
   - Queue growth above the "elastic latency" threshold for N consecutive 10s windows (see SLOs below).
   - GPU preemption or node drain with remaining capacity < 30% of normal pool for more than 60s.
   - Downstream KV cache (>75% miss rate) causing token compute amplification.
2) Request-level triggers:
   - Excessive user-specified max_tokens that would materially violate latency SLO.
   - High cost-of-completion (estimated tokens * model-cost > per-request cap).

High-level fallback sequence (ordered by preference)

1) Prefer prefix/KV cache reuse: route to cached response or partial KV warm path.
2) Model variant swap: same-architectural family, smaller parameterization or higher quantization; ensure semantic parity tests pass.
3) Dynamic decode constraints: reduce max_new_tokens, increase repetition penalties, or switch to nucleus sampling with tuned p.
4) Response shaping / progressive summarization: generate a short summary first and stream longer content if capacity recovers.
5) Rate-limit / backpressure to callers with clear error codes and Retry-After.

Requirements and invariants

- Every fallback must provide an explicit quality-contract label in telemetry (event: fallback.invoked, fields: reason, target_variant, expected_quality_bucket).
- No fallback may silently change the public API surface (response schema must remain stable).
- Fall back to a lower-cost model only when automated A/B tests show degradation within an acceptable delta for that route's guardrail prompt set.
- Fallback transitions must be reversible and bounded: total time on fallback mode should be capped (default 300s) unless an operator extends it.

SLOs and thresholds (recommended defaults)

- Endpoint p95 latency under normal load: <= 350 ms.
- Acceptable degraded mode p95 latency: <= 700 ms.
- Invocation-level fallback rate target: < 0.5% during steady-state; alert at > 2% sustained for 5m.
- Model-swap regression guardrail: ROUGE-L / BLEU / embedding-similarity drop <= 6% on route-specific tests.

Configuration schema (YAML example)

The runtime will consume a canonical per-route fallback policy. Example fields are described below; implementers must validate schema compatibility before rollout.

```
route: /v1/generate/chat
slo_class: latency-sensitive
triggers:
  - type: queue_growth
    threshold_ms: 200
    window_s: 10
  - type: gpu_pool_util
    threshold_pct: 70
fallback_sequence:
  - action: use_kv_cache
    max_age_s: 300
  - action: swap_model
    allowed_variants: ["mistral-7b-q4", "redwood-7b-8q"]
    regression_budget_pct: 6
  - action: reduce_max_tokens
    reduction_pct: 40
  - action: short_summary_then_stream
    summary_length_tokens: 60
timeouts:
  degrade_mode_ttl_s: 300
  per_request_deadline_ms: 1200
```

Operational playbook (runbook)

1) Detection and immediate mitigation (automated):
   - Alerting rule: fallback.rate > 2% for 5m triggers PagerDuty P1 to runtime-sre.
   - Automated response: runtime-gateway marks route as "degrade-mode" and flips routing to fallback_sequence first step.
2) Triage steps (human):
   - Check redwood-console "route-health" dashboard:
     * p95 latency, queue depth, model-pool utilization, KV miss rates.
   - If GPU preemption: open ticket to infra-gpu; if thermal or hardware, follow hardware repair playbook.
   - If degradation due to model regression after an automated rollout: roll back using canary gating (see Rollout section).
3) Recovery and validation:
   - Disable degrade-mode when queue depth and GPU utilization return to normal for 3 consecutive 60s windows.
   - Run route-specific QA prompts and ensure metric deltas are within regression_budget_pct.
   - Post-incident: create a short incident review and append telemetry example events to the regression triage folder.

Rollout and testing guidance

- Canary simulation: run synthetic traffic that matches the route's request size distribution and sequence-length histogram. The simulation must exercise the fallback sequence end-to-end.
- Canary QA checks: compare generation outputs on the guardrail prompt-set and compute embedding-similarity and task-specific metrics.
- Feature flags: degrade-mode toggles must be behind a central feature-flag service with audit logs and automated automatic rollback at SLA thresholds.

Telemetry and dashboards

Essential events and metrics (telemetry names):
- fallback.invoked {route, reason, target_variant, client_id, timestamp}
- route.degrade_mode {route, enabled, trigger_id, ttl_s}
- model.swap.attempt {from_variant, to_variant, success}
- request.quality_estimate {route, request_id, quality_bucket}

Suggested dashboard panels:
- Route fallback-rate (1m/5m/1h), filter by reason and variant.
- Correlation heatmap: queue depth vs. p95 latency vs. fallback invocation.
- Regression dashboard: embedding-similarity delta per guardrail prompt.

API-level contract and error codes

When a route returns a degradation state the public API should still conform to schema and include explicit fields that signal quality and guidance. Example JSON response keys to include:

- "x-redwood-degrade": {"enabled": true, "reason": "high_queue", "expected_quality": "short-summary"}  (embedded in response metadata)
- Error code for enforced backpressure: 429 WITH body {"code": "THROTTLED_DEGRADE", "retry_after_seconds": 12, "message": "Service is temporarily serving a reduced-quality response."}

Implementation notes (runtime responsibilities)

- edge-proxy: must inject route metadata (slo_class, feature_flags) into the request envelope for the runtime-gateway to evaluate.
- runtime-gateway: evaluation layer that executes the fallback policy FSM. It must be idempotent and return a deterministic decision given a stable input.
- model-router: supports transparent model swaps and maintains KV cache affinity when possible.
- batch-orchestrator: used for offloading long completions to a background task; completed results are streamed back when available.

Security and compliance considerations

- Customer-sensitive prompts or PII must not be sent to lower-quota third-party models during fallback in Private deployments. The fallback policy must include a boolean safe_to_fallback flag.
- All fallback decisions and full request/response traces (with redaction) must be retained per the normal audit-logging retention policy.

Operational checklist for owners (pre-deploy)

- [ ] Route-level guardrail prompts defined and stored in prompt-sets-and-benchmarks.
- [ ] Automated tests added to CI: model-swap smoke, summary-first flow, and KV-hits path.
- [ ] Dashboards created and alerting thresholds configured.
- [ ] Feature flag and rollout plan documented with rollback conditions.

Appendix: Example telemetry query (InfluxQL/PromQL style)

- Fallback rate (1m): sum(rate(fallback_invoked_total{route="/v1/generate/chat"}[1m])) / sum(rate(requests_total{route="/v1/generate/chat"}[1m]))
- Degrade-mode duration: sum(increase(route_degrade_mode_seconds_total[1h])) by (route)

References and related pages

- Model slicing and fallback routing guidelines (internal): /confluence/architecture-and-standards/model-serving-standards/model-slicing-and-fallback-routing-guidelines-2026
- Regression triage playbook: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/overview

Contact and ownership

- Primary owner: runtime-platform-team (runtime-gateway group)
- Secondary reviewers: model-onboarding, eng-sre

Change log

- 2025-11-12: Created by Aisha Morgan
- 2026-02-03: Added safe_to_fallback security flag and updated default degrade TTL to 300s (reviewers: Ethan Park, Sofia Martinez)

source.title: ADR: Unified Artifact Delivery Protocol for Multimodal Outputs
source.author: Marina Alvarez
source.body:
Overview:

This ADR defines a unified artifact delivery protocol (UADP v1) for multimodal inference responses across Redwood Inference. The purpose is to provide a single, predictable outbound contract that supports text-only responses, small inline artifacts, and robust streaming/reference-based delivery for large binary artifacts (images, audio, video, documents).

Motivation:

- Customers increasingly request multimodal outputs. Current ad-hoc encodings (inline base64, multiple multipart variants, private-API-only flows) increase SDK complexity, routing edge cases, and storage/caching ambiguities.
- A common protocol helps with caching, tracing, signature verification, and consistent behavior across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.

Scope:

This ADR covers: outbound response envelope, inline vs reference delivery semantics, streaming/resume behavior, checksum and provenance requirements, and a migration strategy. It does NOT mandate transport-level internals (gRPC vs HTTP) but defines headers and JSON envelope expectations.

Goals (concise):

1) One canonical JSON envelope usable for text-only and multimodal outputs.
2) Minimize inline binary usage; prefer streaming/references for >64KB.
3) Built-in checksums and provenance tokens for auditability.
4) Support resumable downloads for large artifacts.

Decision summary:

We adopt UADP version string "uadp.v1". Responses MUST be a JSON object with a top-level field "uadp_version" and an array named "artifacts". Each artifact entry is a flat map of primitive fields. To keep the Confluence document schema flat, the example artifact structure is represented in prose and simple key-value lines below.

Artifact entry fields (all fields are simple string values in the contract):

- artifact_id (required): unique per-response identifier (string).
- artifact_type (required): one of: text, image, audio, file, structured.
- mime_type (recommended): RFC-compliant MIME (e.g., image/png).
- delivery_mode (required): 'inline' or 'reference'.
- size_bytes (optional): decimal string of artifact byte length when known.
- checksum (required for reference, recommended for inline): format algorithm:hex (e.g., sha256:... ).
- provenance_token (optional): opaque string resolving to generation metadata.
- inline_data (when delivery_mode==inline): UTF-8 for text or base64 for binary. Clients SHOULD avoid inline_data for binary > 64KB.
- reference (when delivery_mode==reference): a delivery descriptor string that encodes the reference type and parameters. See Reference types below.

Reference types (reference string format uses a short type prefix and URL-like parameters):

1) s3-presigned://{region}/{bucket}/{key}?expires={rfc3339}
   - Use case: hosted storage objects. Must be short-lived and scope-limited.
2) http-stream://{host}/sessions/{session_id}
   - Use case: chunked streaming endpoint supporting resume. Server must honor Range headers and return resume tokens in header X-Redwood-Resume.
3) attachment-id://{id}
   - Use case: private deployments or internal retrieval. Retrieval requires authenticated internal API or mTLS.

Streaming and resumability (behavioral rules):

- Default threshold for recommending streaming: artifacts >= 5,242,880 bytes (5MB).
- Streaming endpoints MUST allow byte-range resume semantics using standard Range: bytes=<offset>- headers.
- Servers MUST emit an opaque resume token in response header X-Redwood-Resume when a resume session is granted. The token is HMAC-signed and encodes attachment id and offset. Token rotation policy: rotate HMAC keys at least every 90 days.

Inline payload constraints:

- Inline text limit: recommended 262144 bytes (256KB).
- Inline binary limit: hard limit 65536 bytes (64KB). Requests producing larger binary artifacts MUST use reference delivery.

Checksums and provenance:

- Reference deliveries MUST include checksum field. SHA-256 is the preferred algorithm.
- Provenance_token when present must be resolvable to: generation timestamp, model version, quantization/profile, and storage location by internal tooling.

Compatibility and migration approach:

- Legacy text-only clients: UADP allows a compatibility mode where gateways can return a plain text body for Accept: text/plain clients. New clients should request Accept: application/vnd.redwood.uadp.v1+json to get the full envelope.
- Opt-in rollout: 90-day opt-in window where both legacy plain-text and UADP are supported. After that, UADP becomes the default for endpoints that produce non-text artifacts.

Operational defaults (recommended values):

- INLINE_BINARY_LIMIT_BYTES = 65536
- INLINE_TEXT_LIMIT_BYTES = 262144
- STREAMING_THRESHOLD_BYTES = 5242880
- RESUME_SESSION_TTL_HOURS = 24
- PRESIGNED_URL_TTL_SECONDS = 900
- CHECKSUM_ALGORITHM = sha256

Gateway and SDK requirements:

- Gateway: implement translation layer to map older simple responses into UADP envelopes when Accept header requests it; enforce presigned URL generation policies and attach X-Redwood-Resume where applicable.
- SDKs (JS/Python/Go): provide helpers to parse UADP envelopes, transparently follow reference links with resume support, and surface provenance tokens to client applications.

Observability and telemetry:

Emit the following metrics and trace tags:

- metric: uadp.requests.total (labels: delivery_mode, artifact_type)
- metric: uadp.streaming.sessions_active
- metric: uadp.resume.attempts and uadp.resume.failures
- trace tags: uadp.version, uadp.artifact_type, uadp.checksum:last8 (only last 8 hex chars in logs)

Security considerations:

- Presigned URLs must be short-lived and not embed long-term credentials.
- Resume tokens MUST be HMAC-signed with key rotation.
- For private deployments, prefer attachment-id delivery with internal authenticated retrieval APIs and mutual TLS.

Testing and validation plan:

- Unit tests for envelope schema serialization, checksum verification, and token validation.
- Integration tests: resumable download under intermittent connectivity, gateway translation for legacy clients, and SDK resume behavior.
- Performance tests: measure memory and latency impact when serving inline vs reference across 50th, 90th and 95th percentiles.

Trade-offs considered:

- Pros: single contract simplifies clients and enables better caching and auditability.
- Cons: extra implementation work in runtimes and gateways; clients must handle reference-based flows.

Open issues / future work:

- Evaluate a push-based WebSocket protocol for real-time streams in a future UADP v2.
- Define cross-tenant sharing semantics for enterprise customers.
- Consider richer chunk-level checksums for very large artifacts.

Rollout checklist (owners and rough timelines):

- SDK updates (eng-developer-experience): add UADP parsing and streaming helpers — 6 weeks.
- Gateway translation (eng-infra): add compatibility layer and presigned URL policy enforcement — 4 weeks.
- Runtime support (eng-serving-runtime): reference payload generation, streaming session manager, resume token signer — 8 weeks.
- Observability (eng-platform): add metrics and trace tag support — 3 weeks.
- Security review (security): pre-signed URL policy, resume token key management — immediate during implementation.

Example (human-readable pseudo-output) :

uadp_version: uadp.v1
request_id: req_2026_01_abc123
model: gpt-multimodal-1.2
artifacts:
- artifact_id: out_0
  artifact_type: image
  mime_type: image/png
  delivery_mode: reference
  size_bytes: 312452
  checksum: sha256:3a4f...9c
  reference: s3-presigned://us-west-2/mybucket/path/to/object?expires=2026-02-03T15:04:05Z
- artifact_id: out_1
  artifact_type: text
  delivery_mode: inline
  inline_data: A short caption describing the image.

References and related documentation:

- See eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture for implementation notes and sample session manager code.
- SDK guides will appear under eng-developer-experience/sdk-guides.

If you have feedback, add comments to the reviewers list or contact the proposal owner.
