source.title: ADR-021: Private Observability Minimum Pack — Scope, Stack Dependencies, and Export Integrations
source.author: Allison Grant
source.content:
## Status
Published (effective starting Redwood Private release line: 2025.07)

## Context
Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem) deployments have historically had bespoke observability setups depending on the customer’s cluster standards and their preferred tooling. This creates recurring issues:
- Support/SRE cannot rely on consistent signals, dashboards, or alert semantics across installs.
- Customers experience time-to-diagnose delays (missing dashboards, inconsistent metric names, no runbook links, noisy/unbounded alerts).
- Air-gapped/on-prem installs require deterministic packaging of artifacts (dashboards/rules/config), which ad-hoc setups do not provide.

The “Private Observability Minimum Pack” project standardizes the lowest viable, supported observability bundle that ships with Redwood Private via Helm charts and the private installer.

This ADR defines:
- What is in scope vs out of scope for the minimum pack (v1)
- The required dependencies (Prometheus/Grafana baseline) and what “supported” means
- How export integrations (logs/metrics, and limited traces where feasible) are handled in private environments

## Decision summary
1) **Prometheus + Grafana are the supported baseline for the minimum pack** (dashboards + alert rules + recording rules), delivered as versioned artifacts and wired into Redwood’s Helm charts/installer.

2) **The minimum pack is an integration bundle, not a full observability platform.** We provide:
- Redwood metrics endpoints and a curated set of alerts/dashboards
- A recommended deployment path for Prometheus/Grafana (customer-managed or Redwood-provisioned where allowed)
- Log collection defaults and supported exports (see ADR-022)

We do **not** build or operate a Redwood-specific metrics backend, log SIEM, or tracing backend for Private.

3) **Exports are supported via documented configuration paths and tested reference configurations**, not via bespoke per-customer engineering:
- Logs: Loki, Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, Splunk HEC, and optional OTLP logs (where customer has an OTEL collector)
- Metrics: Prometheus remote_write as an integration path (customer-managed destination)
- Traces: best-effort (v1), optional OTLP tracing enablement where runtime components emit spans; no guaranteed end-to-end tracing in v1

4) **Scope is explicitly SLO- and incident-driven:** cover the highest-impact failure modes for inference serving and private control plane health, with bounded cardinality and upgrade-safe artifact versioning.

## Goals (what success looks like)
- A Private install has usable dashboards/alerts within 30 minutes of install/upgrade, without bespoke setup.
- Redwood Support/SRE can ask for a consistent “minimum pack” dashboard/alert set during escalations.
- Air-gapped installs can validate artifacts (dashboards/rules/config/images) are present and versioned.
- Metrics and labels are bounded to avoid customer Prometheus overload.

## Non-goals
- Full “premium observability” (long retention, federation, cross-cluster aggregation, advanced tracing controls, bespoke dashboards).
- Supporting every monitoring stack (Datadog, New Relic, proprietary APMs) in v1.
- Creating a new Redwood-managed SIEM or log storage for Private.

## In scope (v1 minimum pack)
### A) Metrics + alerts
- Standardized metrics naming/labels for Private (see metrics/alerting standards)
- Recording rules for runtime golden signals:
  - Request rate (RPS) and token throughput
  - Error rate (5xx, timeouts, upstream dependency errors)
  - Latency p50/p95/p99 (request + token generation where applicable)
  - Saturation indicators (GPU utilization, queue depth, batch size, cache hit rate)
- Prometheus alert rules mapped to Private SLO guidance:
  - Availability / error budget burn (page-worthy vs ticket-only)
  - Latency SLO burn (p95/p99) and sustained degradation
  - GPU/CPU/memory pressure, OOMKills, restart loops
  - Scrape failures (Prometheus target down), exporter health, TSDB issues (disk filling)

### B) Dashboards (Grafana)
Curated “minimum supported” dashboards (names are normative; exact panel composition may evolve with versions):
- Private Runtime Overview (golden signals + batching/KV cache + GPU panels)
- Control Plane Health (API gateway, routing/policy engine, auth/RBAC integration points, installer/upgrade controller)
- Kubernetes/Infra Summary (node/pod health, persistent volume usage for Prometheus/Grafana, scrape health)

### C) Logs (operational)
- Baseline log collection configuration shipped with the installer/Helm charts
- Structured log fields aligned across control plane and data plane (e.g., request_id, component, severity, model, route; tenant identifier must be hashed)
- Safe-by-default redaction posture (no request payload logging by default; secrets never logged)
- Export configuration patterns for supported sinks (details governed by ADR-022)

### D) Packaging + installation
- Versioned “observability-pack” artifacts (dashboards, rules, configs) pinned to Redwood Private releases
- Installer preflight checks relevant to observability readiness (storage class, network policies, resource requests)
- Post-install validation steps (scrape targets present, dashboards loaded, critical alerts not firing)
- Air-gapped bundle support: all images and artifacts included, with checksums/signing where required by the offline packaging process

## Out of scope (explicitly)
- Shipping a fully managed, Redwood-operated Prometheus/Grafana service inside customer environments.
- Long-term log/metric retention beyond default recommendations (customers can configure retention and storage).
- Multi-cluster federation, Thanos/Cortex/Mimir architecture design and support as part of v1.
- Guaranteed end-to-end distributed tracing across every component.
- Customer-specific dashboard customization as a supported offering (customers may clone dashboards, but it is not supported for troubleshooting unless they reproduce issues on minimum-pack dashboards).

## Options considered
### Option 1: Support multiple monitoring stacks (Prometheus/Grafana + vendor APMs) in v1
**Rejected.** Increases combinatorial support burden and makes “minimum supported” ambiguous. Private customers have highly variable constraints, and supporting multiple vendor-specific solutions would slow delivery and reduce reliability.

### Option 2: Redwood Private ships with kube-prometheus-stack as a hard dependency
**Partially accepted (recommended, not mandatory).**
- We will document and validate against kube-prometheus-stack as the reference installation.
- We will not require customers to run the exact chart if they already have Prometheus/Grafana; however, they must meet compatibility requirements (scraping, Grafana dashboard provisioning, PromQL support).

### Option 3: Use OpenTelemetry Collector + OTLP as the primary standard for everything (metrics/logs/traces)
**Rejected for v1 as the primary baseline.**
- OTLP is a useful integration path, but many Private customers are standardized on Prometheus + Grafana for metrics and alerting.
- Prometheus-native alerting (PromQL) is the fastest path to deliver the minimum pack and to align with existing SRE muscle memory.
- OTLP remains an optional export/integration path (especially for logs and traces) where customers already operate an OTEL pipeline.

## Decision details
### 1) Supported baseline: Prometheus + Grafana
The minimum pack’s dashboards and alert rules are delivered as:
- Grafana dashboards (JSON) provisionable via ConfigMaps or Grafana sidecar provisioning
- PrometheusRule resources (or equivalent rules files) containing:
  - Recording rules (to stabilize dashboard queries and reduce repeated expensive PromQL)
  - Alerting rules with consistent labels and annotations

**Support policy:**
- If a customer uses Prometheus + Grafana (or compatible equivalents) and deploys the minimum pack artifacts unmodified, Redwood Support will treat the signals as canonical.
- If a customer uses alternative stacks, Support will request they enable the Prometheus-compatible endpoints and provide equivalent evidence; best-effort support applies.

### 2) Definition of “minimum supported”
“Minimum supported” means:
- The artifacts are shipped, versioned, and tested in reference environments (VPC + on-prem-like)
- Alert rules include:
  - severity taxonomy (Sev0/Sev1/Sev2 or page/ticket classification per Private on-call policy)
  - runbook links in annotations
  - noise controls (for example: for 5xx, require sustained burn or minimum traffic)
- Metric names/labels are stable across minor releases; breaking changes require documented migration and upgrade checks

### 3) Export integrations (how we handle customer toolchains)
We support exports by providing:
- A default collector configuration (logs) with documented toggles and examples
- A “supported sinks” list with validation coverage and known limitations
- A matrix of prerequisites per sink (network egress, auth, certificates, buffering)

**We do not**:
- Operate or host the destination systems
- Write one-off transformations for customer-specific field mappings beyond documented examples

**Metrics exports:**
- Primary mode is scrape by Prometheus
- Supported integration path: Prometheus remote_write to a customer-managed backend (implementation and operations are customer-owned)

**Logs exports:**
- Governed by ADR-022; minimum pack includes tested reference configs for Splunk HEC and Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, plus Loki as a common in-cluster option

**Traces:**
- v1 is “best effort” and optional; where spans exist, OTLP export may be enabled, but absence of traces is not considered a minimum-pack failure

### 4) Cardinality and privacy constraints
Private environments can have strict resource envelopes. Therefore:
- Labels with high cardinality (tenant identifiers, request_id, raw route/model strings) must be bounded or hashed/normalized.
- Default dashboards/alerts must rely on aggregated signals.
- Any “per-model” or “per-route” breakdown must be limited to allowlists/top-K patterns, not unbounded label explosion.

Security constraints:
- No secrets, API keys, or request prompts/completions in operational logs by default.
- Tenant identifiers must be hashed (non-reversible) unless explicitly enabled by the customer and approved by Security & Compliance.

## Consequences
### Positive
- Clear, repeatable support posture for Private escalations.
- Faster installation validation and fewer “no dashboards/no alerts” incidents.
- Reduced operational variance across VPC and on-prem installs.

### Negative / tradeoffs
- Customers on vendor-only monitoring stacks may require extra integration effort.
- Some advanced visibility (deep tracing, long retention, federation) remains out of scope and may be requested by large enterprises.

### Risks and mitigations
- **Risk:** Alert noise causes customers to disable alerts.
  - **Mitigation:** Ship conservative defaults; separate paging vs ticket alerts; include tuning guidance.
- **Risk:** Prometheus TSDB sizing issues in higher-throughput Private clusters.
  - **Mitigation:** Provide sizing/retention guidance and installer preflight warnings; recommend recording rules.
- **Risk:** Air-gapped artifact drift across versions.
  - **Mitigation:** Version-pin artifacts; include in offline bundle with checksums; add post-install validation.

## Implementation notes (normative requirements)
- Dashboards and rules must be versioned and referenced by the Redwood Private release version.
- Every alert must include:
  - `summary` and `description`
  - `runbook_url`
  - `severity` or `paging` label aligned with Private paging policy
- Installer must provide:
  - Preflight checks: storage class availability, expected ports/NetworkPolicy compatibility, minimal CPU/memory requests
  - Post-install checks: Prometheus targets UP for Redwood services; Grafana dashboards present; rule groups loaded

## Rollout plan
- Ship as opt-out enabled-by-default in new installs.
- For upgrades, enable by default but allow customers to keep existing external Prometheus/Grafana and only import Redwood artifacts.
- Backport critical fixes (dashboards broken, invalid rules, excessive cardinality) to supported Private release branches.

## Open questions (tracked separately)
- Do we standardize on a single “recommended” Helm stack (kube-prometheus-stack + Grafana) as a Redwood-supported deployment profile in a future release?
- Should OTLP traces become a minimum requirement once runtime tracing coverage reaches a defined threshold?
- Should we ship optional Thanos sidecar + object storage guidance as a documented “extended retention” profile (still customer-operated)?

## Appendix: Compatibility requirements (Prometheus/Grafana)
Customers bringing their own stack must support:
- Prometheus scraping Kubernetes service endpoints (TLS optional, configurable)
- PromQL features used by shipped rules/dashboards (recording rules, histogram_quantile where applicable)
- Grafana dashboard provisioning from JSON, and Prometheus datasource access

If these requirements are not met, Redwood Support will request deployment of the reference setup for troubleshooting.

title: ADR-021: Private Observability Minimum Pack — Scope, Stack Dependencies, and Export Integrations
preview:
## Status
Published (effective starting Redwood Private release line: 2025.07)

## Context
Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem) deployments have historically had bespoke observability setups depending on the customer’s cluster standards and their preferred tooling. This creates recurring issues:
- Support/SRE cannot rely on consistent signals, dashboards, or alert semantics across installs.
- Customers experience time-to-diagnose delays (missing dashboards, inconsistent metric names, no runbook links, noisy/unbounded alerts).
- Air-gapped/on-prem installs require deterministic packaging of artifacts (dashboards/rules/config), which ad-hoc setups do not provide.

The “Private Observability Minimum Pack” project standardizes the lowest viable, supported observability bundle that ships with Redwood Private via Helm charts and the private installer.

This ADR defines:
- What is in scope vs out of scope for the minimum pack (v1)
- The required dependencies (Prometheus/Grafana baseline) and what “supported” means
- How export integrations (logs/metrics, and limited traces where feasible) are handled in private environments

## Decision summary
1) **Prometheus + Grafana are the supported baseline for the minimum pack** (dashboards + alert rules + recording rules), delivered as versioned artifacts and wired into Redwood’s Helm charts/installer.

2) **The minimum pack is an integration bundle, not a full observability platform.** We provide:
- Redwood metrics endpoints and a curated set of alerts/dashboards
- A recommended deployment path for Prometheus/Grafana (customer-managed or Redwood-provisioned where allowed)
- Log collection defaults and supported exports (see ADR-022)

We do **not** build or operate a Redwood-specific metrics backend, log SIEM, or tracing backend for Private.

3) **Exports are supported via documented configuration paths and tested reference configurations**, not via bespoke per-customer engineering:
- Logs: Loki, Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, Splunk HEC, and optional OTLP logs (where customer has an OTEL collector)
- Metrics: Prometheus remote_write as an integration path (customer-managed destination)
- Traces: best-effort (v1), optional OTLP tracing enablement where runtime components emit spans; no guaranteed end-to-end tracing in v1

4) **Scope is explicitly SLO- and incident-driven:** cover the highest-impact failure modes for inference serving and private control plane health, with bounded cardinality and upgrade-safe artifact versioning.

## Goals (what success looks like)
- A Private install has usable dashboards/alerts within 30 minutes of install/upgrade, without bespoke setup.
- Redwood Support/SRE can ask for a consistent “minimum pack” dashboard/alert set during escalations.
- Air-gapped installs can validate artifacts (dashboards/rules/config/images) are present and versioned.
- Metrics and labels are bounded to avoid customer Prometheus overload.

## Non-goals
- Full “premium observability” (long retention, federation, cross-cluster aggregation, advanced tracing controls, bespoke dashboards).
- Supporting every monitoring stack (Datadog, New Relic, proprietary APMs) in v1.
- Creating a new Redwood-managed SIEM or log storage for Private.

## In scope (v1 minimum pack)
### A) Metrics + alerts
- Standardized metrics naming/labels for Private (see metrics/alerting standards)
- Recording rules for runtime golden signals:
  - Request rate (RPS) and token throughput
  - Error rate (5xx, timeouts, upstream dependency errors)
  - Latency p50/p95/p99 (request + token generation where applicable)
  - Saturation indicators (GPU utilization, queue depth, batch size, cache hit rate)
- Prometheus alert rules mapped to Private SLO guidance:
  - Availability / error budget burn (page-worthy vs ticket-only)
  - Latency SLO burn (p95/p99) and sustained degradation
  - GPU/CPU/memory pressure, OOMKills, restart loops
  - Scrape failures (Prometheus target down), exporter health, TSDB issues (disk filling)

### B) Dashboards (Grafana)
Curated “minimum supported” dashboards (names are normative; exact panel composition may evolve with versions):
- Private Runtime Overview (golden signals + batching/KV cache + GPU panels)
- Control Plane Health (API gateway, routing/policy engine, auth/RBAC integration points, installer/upgrade controller)
- Kubernetes/Infra Summary (node/pod health, persistent volume usage for Prometheus/Grafana, scrape health)

### C) Logs (operational)
- Baseline log collection configuration shipped with the installer/Helm charts
- Structured log fields aligned across control plane and data plane (e.g., request_id, component, severity, model, route; tenant identifier must be hashed)
- Safe-by-default redaction posture (no request payload logging by default; secrets never logged)
- Export configuration patterns for supported sinks (details governed by ADR-022)

### D) Packaging + installation
- Versioned “observability-pack” artifacts (dashboards, rules, configs) pinned to Redwood Private releases
- Installer preflight checks relevant to observability readiness (storage class, network policies, resource requests)
- Post-install validation steps (scrape targets present, dashboards loaded, critical alerts not firing)
- Air-gapped bundle support: all images and artifacts included, with checksums/signing where required by the offline packaging process

## Out of scope (explicitly)
- Shipping a fully managed, Redwood-operated Prometheus/Grafana service inside customer environments.
- Long-term log/metric retention beyond default recommendations (customers can configure retention and storage).
- Multi-cluster federation, Thanos/Cortex/Mimir architecture design and support as part of v1.
- Guaranteed end-to-end distributed tracing across every component.
- Customer-specific dashboard customization as a supported offering (customers may clone dashboards, but it is not supported for troubleshooting unless they reproduce issues on minimum-pack dashboards).

## Options considered
### Option 1: Support multiple monitoring stacks (Prometheus/Grafana + vendor APMs) in v1
**Rejected.** Increases combinatorial support burden and makes “minimum supported” ambiguous. Private customers have highly variable constraints, and supporting multiple vendor-specific solutions would slow delivery and reduce reliability.

### Option 2: Redwood Private ships with kube-prometheus-stack as a hard dependency
**Partially accepted (recommended, not mandatory).**
- We will document and validate against kube-prometheus-stack as the reference installation.
- We will not require customers to run the exact chart if they already have Prometheus/Grafana; however, they must meet compatibility requirements (scraping, Grafana dashboard provisioning, PromQL support).

### Option 3: Use OpenTelemetry Collector + OTLP as the primary standard for everything (metrics/logs/traces)
**Rejected for v1 as the primary baseline.**
- OTLP is a useful integration path, but many Private customers are standardized on Prometheus + Grafana for metrics and alerting.
- Prometheus-native alerting (PromQL) is the fastest path to deliver the minimum pack and to align with existing SRE muscle memory.
- OTLP remains an optional export/integration path (especially for logs and traces) where customers already operate an OTEL pipeline.

## Decision details
### 1) Supported baseline: Prometheus + Grafana
The minimum pack’s dashboards and alert rules are delivered as:
- Grafana dashboards (JSON) provisionable via ConfigMaps or Grafana sidecar provisioning
- PrometheusRule resources (or equivalent rules files) containing:
  - Recording rules (to stabilize dashboard queries and reduce repeated expensive PromQL)
  - Alerting rules with consistent labels and annotations

**Support policy:**
- If a customer uses Prometheus + Grafana (or compatible equivalents) and deploys the minimum pack artifacts unmodified, Redwood Support will treat the signals as canonical.
- If a customer uses alternative stacks, Support will request they enable the Prometheus-compatible endpoints and provide equivalent evidence; best-effort support applies.

### 2) Definition of “minimum supported”
“Minimum supported” means:
- The artifacts are shipped, versioned, and tested in reference environments (VPC + on-prem-like)
- Alert rules include:
  - severity taxonomy (Sev0/Sev1/Sev2 or page/ticket classification per Private on-call policy)
  - runbook links in annotations
  - noise controls (for example: for 5xx, require sustained burn or minimum traffic)
- Metric names/labels are stable across minor releases; breaking changes require documented migration and upgrade checks

### 3) Export integrations (how we handle customer toolchains)
We support exports by providing:
- A default collector configuration (logs) with documented toggles and examples
- A “supported sinks” list with validation coverage and known limitations
- A matrix of prerequisites per sink (network egress, auth, certificates, buffering)

**We do not**:
- Operate or host the destination systems
- Write one-off transformations for customer-specific field mappings beyond documented examples

**Metrics exports:**
- Primary mode is scrape by Prometheus
- Supported integration path: Prometheus remote_write to a customer-managed backend (implementation and operations are customer-owned)

**Logs exports:**
- Governed by ADR-022; minimum pack includes tested reference configs for Splunk HEC and Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, plus Loki as a common in-cluster option

**Traces:**
- v1 is “best effort” and optional; where spans exist, OTLP export may be enabled, but absence of traces is not considered a minimum-pack failure

### 4) Cardinality and privacy constraints
Private environments can have strict resource envelopes. Therefore:
- Labels with high cardinality (tenant identifiers, request_id, raw route/model strings) must be bounded or hashed/normalized.
- Default dashboards/alerts must rely on aggregated signals.
- Any “per-model” or “per-route” breakdown must be limited to allowlists/top-K patterns, not unbounded label explosion.

Security constraints:
- No secrets, API keys, or request prompts/completions in operational logs by default.
- Tenant identifiers must be hashed (non-reversible) unless explicitly enabled by the customer and approved by Security & Compliance.

## Consequences
### Positive
- Clear, repeatable support posture for Private escalations.
- Faster installation validation and fewer “no dashboards/no alerts” incidents.
- Reduced operational variance across VPC and on-prem installs.

### Negative / tradeoffs
- Customers on vendor-only monitoring stacks may require extra integration effort.
- Some advanced visibility (deep tracing, long retention, federation) remains out of scope and may be requested by large enterprises.

### Risks and mitigations
- **Risk:** Alert noise causes customers to disable alerts.
  - **Mitigation:** Ship conservative defaults; separate paging vs ticket alerts; include tuning guidance.
- **Risk:** Prometheus TSDB sizing issues in higher-throughput Private clusters.
  - **Mitigation:** Provide sizing/retention guidance and installer preflight warnings; recommend recording rules.
- **Risk:** Air-gapped artifact drift across versions.
  - **Mitigation:** Version-pin artifacts; include in offline bundle with checksums; add post-install validation.

## Implementation notes (normative requirements)
- Dashboards and rules must be versioned and referenced by the Redwood Private release version.
- Every alert must include:
  - `summary` and `description`
  - `runbook_url`
  - `severity` or `paging` label aligned with Private paging policy
- Installer must provide:
  - Preflight checks: storage class availability, expected ports/NetworkPolicy compatibility, minimal CPU/memory requests
  - Post-install checks: Prometheus targets UP for Redwood services; Grafana dashboards present; rule groups loaded

## Rollout plan
- Ship as opt-out enabled-by-default in new installs.
- For upgrades, enable by default but allow customers to keep existing external Prometheus/Grafana and only import Redwood artifacts.
- Backport critical fixes (dashboards broken, invalid rules, excessive cardinality) to supported Private release branches.

## Open questions (tracked separately)
- Do we standardize on a single “recommended” Helm stack (kube-prometheus-stack + Grafana) as a Redwood-supported deployment profile in a future release?
- Should OTLP traces become a minimum requirement once runtime tracing coverage reaches a defined threshold?
- Should we ship optional Thanos sidecar + object storage guidance as a documented “extended retention” profile (still customer-operated)?

## Appendix: Compatibility requirements (Prometheus/Grafana)
Customers bringing their own stack must support:
- Prometheus scraping Kubernetes service endpoints (TLS optional, configurable)
- PromQL features used by shipped rules/dashboards (recording rules, histogram_quantile where applicable)

source.title: Burst policy, SLA smoothing, and simulated loadbook
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.body:
SUMMARY
This playbook describes a unified approach for handling traffic bursts against latency SLOs using three coordinated mechanisms: (1) burst admission policies, (2) SLA smoothing (adaptive target windows and error-budget shaping), and (3) simulated-load scenarios for verification and capacity planning. The goal is to keep P95 and P99 latency SLOs predictable across short-term spikes while limiting cost and preserving error budgets for sustained load.

Scope and audience
- Scope: inference-router, cost-aware-batcher, model-variant-fallback, and the traffic-shaper service.
- Audience: Platform SREs, Serving Runtime, Performance Engineering, and Product engineers owning route-level SLOs.

Motivation
Large variance in upstream request arrival patterns (SDK retries, client-side misconfiguration, bursty user behavior) causes repeated SLO breaches even when average utilization is within capacity. Uncoordinated burst handling either causes degraded user experience (drop/queue overflows) or large cost spikes (over-provisioning). This playbook prescribes deterministic, observable mechanisms that trade short-term admission for long-term stability.

Key concepts
1) Burst window: short interval (configurable, default 10s) where a higher-than-normal arrival rate is expected.
2) Burst budget: token-bucket capacity assigned per tenant/route that permits short bursts without immediate throttling.
3) Smoothing window: sliding window used to compute adaptive SLO targets (default 5m).
4) Admission tiers: fast-path (low latency), slow-path (batched/queued), and reject path.

SLOs and target numbers (examples)
- Global routing SLO: P95 latency <= 120ms, P99 <= 350ms for synchronous text-generation routes.
- Error budget: 0.1% of requests per 30d window.
- Burst policy defaults: burst window = 10s, burst budget = 500 tokens/tenant, refill rate = 50 tokens/s.
- Slow-path threshold: queueing allowed if estimated added latency < 50ms, otherwise reject.

Service-level roles and ownership
- inference-router: owns admission decisions and tenant-level burst accounting (owner: eng-platform).
- cost-aware-batcher: manages slow-path batching and dynamic batch sizing (owner: eng-serving-runtime).
- traffic-shaper: implements smoothing window and adaptive target adjustments (owner: eng-sre).
- monitor-slo: alerting and dashboards (owner: eng-sre).

Policy rules (operational)
1) Per-tenant burst budgets are allocated at tenant creation and can be tuned by SRE based on contract tier.
2) Admission algorithm (pseudocode):
   a) if tokens_available(tenant) > request_cost then admit to fast-path and decrement tokens.
   b) else if estimated_slow_path_added_latency(request) < slow_path_threshold then place in slow-path queue.
   c) else reject with HTTP 429 and include Retry-After header.
3) Token refill: tokens are refilled at refill_rate but capped by tenant burst budget. Suspicious sustained usage that exhausts refill for > 2m triggers capacity review.
4) Retry and idempotency: clients must include idempotency keys for safe retry behavior; routers deduplicate by idempotency key for 60s.

SLA smoothing algorithm (high level)
- Purpose: prevent SLO churn from brief spikes by adjusting the effective SLO target using a time-weighted average of observed latency with exponential decay.
- Implementation parameters: smoothing_alpha = 0.25 (configurable), smoothing_window = 5m. The traffic-shaper computes smoothed_p95 = alpha * current_p95 + (1-alpha) * previous_smoothed_p95.
- When smoothed_p95 approaches 90% of configured SLO for N consecutive samples (N default 3), the system triggers an adaptive throttle to reduce admission for new fast-path requests by a configured fraction (default 20%).

Interaction with cost-aware batching and fallback
- When smoothing reduces fast-path admission, cost-aware-batcher increases slow-path batch sizes (within latency guardrails) to amortize GPU utilization. Batcher target batch size grows by up to 2x over 30s with safeguards to avoid KV cache misses.
- If slow-path queue backlog exceeds a configured backlog_limit (default 200 reqs/route), the router begins rejecting non-critical traffic classes and failing over to lower-cost model variants for best-effort responses.

Simulation scenarios (must-run test suite)
- Scenario A: Sudden 10x spike for 20s from 1% of tenants. Verify P95 and P99 remain within < 1.1x SLO and error budget consumption does not exceed baseline projection.
- Scenario B: Gradual ramp (x2 per minute) for 10 minutes across all tenants. Verify adaptive throttle triggers and batcher adjusts batch sizes without sustained SLO breaches.
- Scenario C: Synthetic retry storm (clients retry 3x with jitter). Verify idempotency dedupe and that retry throttles prevent cascade.
- Scenario D: Region failover traffic surge (traffic-shaping disabled). Validate fallback model variant usage and on-call escalation triggers.

Simulator configuration and sample command lines
- Simulator: redwood-loadsim v0.9 (internal tool). Key flags: --tenants N --pattern {burst,ramp,retry,failover} --duration s --qps-target per-tenant.
- Example: simulate a 10x 20s burst across 50 tenants:
  redwood-loadsim --tenants 50 --pattern burst --multiplier 10 --burst-window 20 --duration 60 --qps-target 5

- Admission debug mode (runtime):
  curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" 
       "https://inference-router.internal/_debug/admit?tenant=tenant-123&recent=60"
  Response includes: tokens_available, estimated_added_latency_ms, slow_path_queue_length, last_admit_action.

Verification checks (metrics and dashboards)
- Essential metrics: fastpath_admit_rate, slowpath_enqueue_rate, reject_rate(429), p95_latency(route), p99_latency(route), burst_tokens_used, batch_size_histogram, kv_cache_hit_rate.
- Dashboards: one traffic-shaper overview per region, with a tenant heatmap for burst token consumption.
- Alerts:
  - P95 > SLO for 3m AND reject_rate > 0.1% -> Sev2 (oncall eng-sre).
  - burst_tokens_exhausted for 5% of premium tenants -> Paging (Platform SRE).
  - slowpath_queue_length > backlog_limit for 2m -> Alert to owning product + scale-runbook.

Runbook: immediate steps for an oncall engineer
1) Confirm alerts and scope by tenant and region.
2) Run admission debug endpoint for top-5 affected tenants.
3) If tokens are exhausted and traffic is legitimate: increase refill_rate for the tenant temporarily (via admin CLI), notify product for possible rate-limit changes.
4) If traffic appears malicious or retry-based: enable per-route retry-cutoff at router layer and apply network-level mitigation.
5) If slow-path queue backlog persists: scale cost-aware-batcher workers by +25% and monitor KV cache hit-rate.
6) If P99 remains above threshold after 10 minutes: execute failover to fallback model variant and follow escalation path in incident response doc.

Configuration examples (YAML snippets)
- admission-policy (example):
  tenant_default_burst_budget: 500
  tenant_default_refill_rate: 50 # tokens/s
  burst_window_seconds: 10
  slow_path_threshold_ms: 50

- smoothing-config (example):
  smoothing_alpha: 0.25
  smoothing_window_seconds: 300
  adaptive_throttle_fraction: 0.2
  consecutive_samples_threshold: 3

- batcher-config (example):
  min_batch_size: 8
  max_batch_size: 256
  batch_grow_factor: 2.0
  batch_grow_window_seconds: 30

Testing and release guidance
- All changes to admission-policy, smoothing-config, and batcher-config require:
  1) Unit tests for algorithmic behavior.
  2) Integration tests running redwood-loadsim scenarios A and B in staging.
  3) Canary rollout to 2% of tenants followed by 1h observation window.
  4) Gradual ramp to 25% after successful canary, then to 100% after 24h without SLO regressions.

Exceptions and tuning guidelines
- Premium enterprise tenants may request larger burst budgets; these must be gated by a capacity commitment or reserved GPU pool allocation.
- For low-latency routes (e.g., <40ms P95 target), slow-path is disabled; instead we scale fast-path capacity or use pre-warmed inference pools.

Security and privacy considerations
- Token accounting tables are stored in a tenant-scoped Redis cluster with encryption at rest. Access is RBAC-restricted to platform services. Audit logs are required for any manual refill operations.

Appendix A — sample incident timeline (hypothetical)
- 10:01 UTC: sudden spike detected; smoothing_p95 hits 85% SLO.
- 10:03 UTC: adaptive throttle reduces fast-path admission by 20%; slow-path enqueue increases.
- 10:07 UTC: batcher scales batch size; KV cache hit rate drops 6% triggering cache pre-warm job.
- 10:15 UTC: backlog clears and smoothing_p95 decays back under threshold.

Appendix B — FAQ
Q: Why not always reject above capacity?
A: Immediate rejection prevents tail latency degradation but harms product UX and can waste retries. Controlled admission + slow-path batching reduces repeated retries and overall cost.

Q: How should product owners set burst budgets?
A: Start with defaults; increase budgets only after reviewing traffic profile and committing capacity. For critical customer SLAs, recommend reserved capacity in Dedicated.

Related documents and references
- See traffic-smoothing-and-elastic-latency-budgets-standards (performance-standards) for formal SLO math.
- See runtime-sidecar-contract-for-private-deployments-adr for private-deploy interaction patterns.

Owners and contact
- Playbook owner: eng-sre (oncall@redwood.internal)
- Implementation owners: eng-platform (inference-router), eng-serving-runtime (cost-aware-batcher)

Change history
- 2024-11-12: Initial draft by Priya Natarajan.
- 2025-02-18: Added simulator commands and canary rollout steps (reviewed by Marco Ruiz).
- 2025-06-02: Tuned default burst budget/refill and clarified retry dedupe window (reviewed by Anika Shah).

title: Burst policy, SLA smoothing, and simulated loadbook

SUMMARY
This playbook describes a unified approach for handling traffic bursts against latency SLOs using three coordinated mechanisms: (1) burst admission policies, (2) SLA smoothing (adaptive target windows and error-budget shaping), and (3) simulated-load scenarios for verification and capacity planning. The goal is to keep P95 and P99 latency SLOs predictable across short-term spikes while limiting cost and preserving error budgets for sustained load.

Scope and audience
- Scope: inference-router, cost-aware-batcher, model-variant-fallback, and the traffic-shaper service.
- Audience: Platform SREs, Serving Runtime, Performance Engineering, and Product engineers owning route-level SLOs.

Motivation
Large variance in upstream request arrival patterns (SDK retries, client-side misconfiguration, bursty user behavior) causes repeated SLO breaches even when average utilization is within capacity. Uncoordinated burst handling either causes degraded user experience (drop/queue overflows) or large cost spikes (over-provisioning). This playbook prescribes deterministic, observable mechanisms that trade short-term admission for long-term stability.

1) Burst window: short interval (configurable, default 10s) where a higher-than-normal arrival rate is expected.
2) Burst budget: token-bucket capacity assigned per tenant/route that permits short bursts without immediate throttling.

source.title: ADR: Usage + Intent Signals Schema v1 (Canonical PLG Signal Object)
source.author: Logan Wright
source.body:
## Status
**Accepted (v1).** Implemented in the signal aggregation + routing service and used for HubSpot routing.

## Context
Redwood is implementing a privacy-aware pipeline that detects product-led signals (usage thresholds + buying intent), scores them, and routes qualified items into HubSpot for follow-up.

We need a **canonical, versioned signal object** that:
- Works for both **threshold** signals (e.g., spend velocity, sustained tokens, 429 rate) and **intent** signals (e.g., Dedicated/Private docs interest, security FAQ views).
- Supports **aggregation windows** (daily/weekly/30d), **deduping**, and **suppression** (to reduce spam).
- Allows deterministic **scoring** + routing, with auditability.
- Minimizes sensitive data, aligning with the PLG signal data handling policy.

## Decision
Adopt a single canonical record called **PLG Signal (schema v1)** emitted by the scoring service and persisted in analytics storage. The router uses this object to:
- Update HubSpot company properties (latest score/type + history string)
- Create a HubSpot timeline note
- Create a follow-up task (when qualified)

The canonical record is **one row per (org, signal_type, window)** (with defined exceptions for multi-dimension signals described below).

## Definitions
- **Org**: Redwood customer organization (billing/account boundary). Primary entity for scoring and routing.
- **Workspace**: Sub-entity under an org; may exist for multi-team usage.
- **Signal Category**: 
  - **threshold** = derived from product usage / reliability / spend patterns
  - **intent** = derived from behavior that indicates buying evaluation (docs, security pages, pricing interactions)
- **Signal Type**: specific reason for outreach (e.g., `usage_tokens_sustained`, `reliability_429s_repeated`, `intent_private_docs_interest`).

## Canonical schema (v1)
The PLG Signal record is represented in protobuf/JSON with the following fields.

### Required fields
| Field | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `schema_version` | string | Fixed to `1.0` for this ADR. See Versioning section. |
| `signal_id` | string | Stable unique ID for the record. Deterministically derived from `dedupe_key` (recommended) or UUID (allowed). |
| `dedupe_key` | string | Deterministic key used for idempotency and update/overwrite behavior. |
| `org_id` | string | Internal org identifier (primary routing entity). |
| `signal_category` | enum | `threshold` \| `intent`. |
| `signal_type` | enum | See Signal Type enum table below. |
| `window_type` | enum | `daily` \| `rolling_7d` \| `rolling_30d`. |
| `window_start` | string | ISO-8601 timestamp (UTC). Start of aggregation window. |
| `window_end` | string | ISO-8601 timestamp (UTC). End of aggregation window (exclusive). |
| `observed_at` | string | ISO-8601 timestamp when computed (UTC). |
| `score` | int | 0–100 (integer). |
| `confidence` | float | 0.0–1.0. Indicates data completeness + signal reliability. |
| `severity` | enum | `low` \| `medium` \| `high` \| `critical`. |
| `qualified` | bool | Output of qualification gate (score + confidence + suppression checks). |
| `primary_reason` | string | Human-readable explanation intended for HubSpot note/task (no PII). |
| `signal_source_set` | list<string> | One or more sources that contributed (e.g., `telemetry`, `billing`, `docs_analytics`). |

### Optional fields (recommended)
| Field | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `workspace_id` | string | Populated when the signal is naturally tied to a workspace (e.g., workspace creation spikes). For most usage/spend signals, keep org-level. |
| `environment` | enum | `prod` \| `staging` \| `unknown`. Defaults to `prod` when inferred. |
| `region` | string | Region label for routing context (e.g., `na`, `emea`, `apac`) derived from billing country / usage region. |
| `account_tier` | enum | `self_serve` \| `startup` \| `enterprise` (if known). |
| `metrics_json` | string | Compact JSON string of numeric metrics used for scoring (must remain non-PII). |
| `related_urls` | list<string> | For intent signals: canonical doc/pricing URLs (no querystrings; strip user identifiers). |
| `model_family` | string | For model-specific signals when relevant (e.g., repeated OOM on a model family). Use broad family labels, not prompts. |
| `suppressed_until` | string | ISO-8601 timestamp. If set, router must not create a new task before this time. |
| `suppression_reason` | string | E.g., `recent_outreach`, `false_positive`, `marketing_nurture_active`, `existing_csm_ownership`. |
| `last_routed_at` | string | ISO-8601 timestamp when last successfully routed to HubSpot. |
| `hubspot_company_id` | string | Populated by router after lookup/upsert. |
| `hubspot_task_id` | string | Populated when a follow-up task is created. |
| `routing_state` | enum | `not_routed` \| `routed_note_only` \| `routed_task_created` \| `routing_failed`. |
| `routing_error` | string | Truncated error summary (no secrets) when failures occur. |

### `signal_type` enum (v1)
We standardize signal types to keep scoring/routing consistent and avoid dashboard fragmentation.

**Threshold (usage/reliability/expansion) signal types**
- `usage_tokens_sustained` — sustained high token volume over rolling window
- `usage_tokens_spike` — sharp increase vs prior baseline
- `spend_velocity_increasing` — spend increasing week-over-week above cut line
- `reliability_429s_repeated` — repeated rate limiting events (429) above threshold
- `reliability_latency_regression` — p95 latency regression vs baseline (where available)
- `expansion_new_workspaces_spike` — new workspaces created in window
- `expansion_org_invite_spike` — spike in org invites/seat growth (where tracked)

**Intent (evaluation/compliance) signal types**
- `intent_dedicated_docs_interest` — Dedicated docs viewed repeatedly
- `intent_private_docs_interest` — Private/VPC/on-prem docs viewed
- `intent_security_faq_interest` — security/SOC2/retention pages viewed
- `intent_pricing_page_interest` — pricing/packaging page interactions
- `intent_api_key_rotation_event` — key rotation or security hygiene actions suggesting productionization

Notes:
- Signal types are intentionally coarse. Any additional detail should go into `metrics_json` and `primary_reason`.
- New signal types require an update to this ADR (or a v1.x addendum) and a controlled rollout.

## Time windows and aggregation rules
We support three window types in v1:
- `daily`: previous UTC day (00:00–24:00) for rate-like measures and anomaly detection
- `rolling_7d`: last 7*24h for sustained usage and reliability patterns
- `rolling_30d`: last 30*24h for longer-term upgrades and enterprise readiness

**Window selection guidance**
- Reliability signals: compute both `daily` and `rolling_7d`. Use `rolling_7d` for qualification; keep `daily` for context.
- Spend/usage upgrades: use `rolling_7d` for velocity + `rolling_30d` for sustained volume.
- Intent signals: use `rolling_7d` to reduce vanity noise while keeping the data fresh.

**Late-arriving data / backfills**
- Aggregator is allowed to recompute windows for up to **7 days** back (telemetry lag/backfill).
- Recomputes MUST use the same `dedupe_key` so downstream consumers update existing records idempotently.

## Dedupe keys (idempotency)
Dedupe is required to prevent HubSpot spam and to allow safe retries.

### Default dedupe key
For most signals:

`dedupe_key = "v1|" + org_id + "|" + signal_type + "|" + window_type + "|" + window_start + "|" + window_end`

### Multi-dimension exception (intent URLs)
For intent signals where the specific page family matters, we allow a controlled dimension:
- Only for `intent_*_docs_interest` and `intent_security_faq_interest`
- Dimension is `canonical_url_group` (e.g., `docs/dedicated/*`, `docs/private/*`, `security/*`)

`dedupe_key = "v1|" + org_id + "|" + signal_type + "|" + canonical_url_group + "|" + window_type + "|" + window_start + "|" + window_end`

Rules:
- Do **not** include raw URLs with query parameters.
- Do **not** include user identifiers.

### Multi-dimension exception (workspace-scoped expansion)
For `expansion_new_workspaces_spike` and `expansion_org_invite_spike`, `workspace_id` may be included if the behavior is workspace-local and actionable.

## Event sources and normalization
Signals are computed from the following source streams.

### Sources (v1)
1) **Product telemetry** (`telemetry`)
- request events: tokens in/out, model, latency, status codes
- error events: 429s, 5xx, timeouts
- aggregation joins: org_id/workspace_id mapping (internal)

2) **Billing & metering** (`billing`)
- spend per day/week, plan type, credit consumption (no payment method details)

3) **Docs & website analytics** (`docs_analytics`)
- page view counts by canonical path group
- no raw IPs stored in signal object
- no per-user identities included in canonical signal

4) **Security-related product events** (`security_events`)
- API key rotation event counts
- SSO/SAML configuration events (future; not used for qualification in v1)

### Normalization requirements
- All sources must map to `org_id` (required).
- `workspace_id` is optional and only used where it changes the action.
- Canonicalization of URLs is required: strip querystrings; map to a small set of path groups.

## Scoring approach (v1)
We use a transparent, rules-based score (0–100). The score is used for:
- qualification gating
- prioritization (severity)
- reporting and tuning

### Score components
**1) Magnitude (0–60)**
- how far above threshold the metric is

**2) Persistence (0–25)**
- whether the signal repeats across windows (e.g., 2+ days in last 7)

**3) Intent boost (0–15)**
- applies only when both threshold + intent are present for the org within 7 days

### Confidence
`confidence` reflects data quality and completeness:
- telemetry coverage for the window (e.g., ingestion lag, missing partitions)
- docs analytics availability
- mapping completeness for org/workspace

Routing and task creation must require:
- `confidence >= 0.70`

### Qualification gate (default)
A signal is considered qualified when:
- `score >= 70`
- `confidence >= 0.70`
- `suppressed_until` is absent or in the past
- org is not in internal/test denylist

**Severity mapping**
- `critical`: score 90–100
- `high`: 80–89
- `medium`: 70–79
- `low`: <70 (stored for analytics but not routed as tasks)

## Privacy and data minimization constraints
The canonical signal object MUST NOT include:
- prompts, completions, message content
- end-user identifiers
- raw IP addresses
- full URLs with query parameters (may include user/session identifiers)

Allowed:
- aggregated counts/sums (tokens, spend, error counts)
- canonical URL path groups (e.g., `docs/private/*`)
- coarse region/tier labels

See: PLG Signal Data Handling & Retention policy for retention and deletion requirements.

## Example payload (illustrative)
```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "signal_id": "sig_01HZXE8K2Y...",
  "dedupe_key": "v1|org_9f3...|reliability_429s_repeated|rolling_7d|2025-05-22T00:00:00Z|2025-05-29T00:00:00Z",
  "org_id": "org_9f3...",
  "signal_category": "threshold",
  "signal_type": "reliability_429s_repeated",
  "window_type": "rolling_7d",
  "window_start": "2025-05-22T00:00:00Z",
  "window_end": "2025-05-29T00:00:00Z",
  "observed_at": "2025-05-29T03:12:44Z",
  "score": 86,
  "confidence": 0.92,
  "severity": "high",
  "qualified": true,
  "primary_reason": "429 rate limiting observed on 4 of last 7 days; token volume up 2.1x WoW. Candidate for Dedicated capacity to stabilize throughput.",
  "signal_source_set": ["telemetry", "billing"],
  "metrics_json": "{\"http_429_count\":1432,\"active_days_with_429\":4,\"tokens_7d\":182340000,\"tokens_wow_growth\":2.1}"
}
```

## Versioning strategy
We version the schema with a `schema_version` string.

Rules:
- **Additive changes** (new optional fields, new enum values) increment **minor**: `1.1`, `1.2`, etc.
- **Breaking changes** (rename/remove fields, change meaning/units) increment **major**: `2.0`.
- Router and HubSpot mappings must accept **all 1.x** payloads.
- `signal_type` expansions are allowed in v1.x but require:
  - updated documentation (this ADR addendum)
  - dashboards updated to include new type
  - a pilot cohort run to validate noise levels

## Consequences
### Positive
- Single schema simplifies:
  - aggregation jobs
  - scoring logic
  - HubSpot routing and reporting
- Deterministic dedupe keys make retries safe and reduce noise.
- Clear privacy constraints prevent accidental leakage of sensitive content into CRM.

### Negative / tradeoffs
- Standardizing `signal_type` reduces flexibility for one-off experiments.
- A coarse schema may require `metrics_json` to carry additional context; must be disciplined to keep it non-PII and compact.

## Alternatives considered
1) **Separate schemas per signal family (usage vs intent vs reliability)**
- Pros: tailored fields, potentially clearer per domain
- Cons: downstream routing becomes brittle; more HubSpot mapping work; harder to audit across signals
- Decision: rejected in favor of one canonical record.

2) **Store raw events directly in HubSpot (timeline events per occurrence)**
- Pros: minimal internal storage
- Cons: HubSpot rate limits; high noise; privacy risk; hard to aggregate and score reliably
- Decision: rejected.

3) **Maintain bespoke tables only in analytics, with router-specific joins**
- Pros: quick initial iteration
- Cons: implicit contracts; difficult to coordinate changes; router becomes tightly coupled to upstream tables
- Decision: rejected.

4) **ML-based lead scoring model**
- Pros: could improve precision/recall over time
- Cons: requires labeled outcomes; harder to explain; slower iteration; governance overhead
- Decision: defer. Start with transparent rules-based scoring; revisit after collecting outcomes.

## Open questions (tracked for v1.x)
- Should we add `baseline_window` fields to make anomaly signals more interpretable (e.g., compare to prior 7d)?
- Should we standardize a `signal_group_id` to cluster multiple signals for a single org into one recommended action?
- Should we add a first-class `recommended_motion` enum (`hosted_to_dedicated`, `hosted_to_private`, `security_review`, `performance_poc`) rather than inferring from type?

## Implementation notes
- Aggregation job computes metrics and writes a normalized intermediate table.
- Scoring service produces PLG Signal objects and persists them to the `plg_signals_v1` table.
- Router consumes qualified signals and performs HubSpot upserts with idempotency anchored on `dedupe_key`.

Ownership:
- Schema + scoring: Telemetry & Analytics (Logan Wright / Mei Lin)
- Router + HubSpot integration: Platform (Priya Natarajan / Ethan Park) + BizOps (David Kim)

Change process:
- v1.x changes require review by Product (Nikhil Sharma) and BizOps (David Kim) and Security sign-off if new fields touch customer data categories.

title: ADR: Usage + Intent Signals Schema v1 (Canonical PLG Signal Object)

## Status
**Accepted (v1).** Implemented in the signal aggregation + routing service and used for HubSpot routing.

## Context
Redwood is implementing a privacy-aware pipeline that detects product-led signals (usage thresholds + buying intent), scores them, and routes qualified items into HubSpot for follow-up.

We need a **canonical, versioned signal object** that:
- Works for both **threshold** signals (e.g., spend velocity, sustained tokens, 429 rate) and **intent** signals (e.g., Dedicated/Private docs interest, security FAQ views).
- Supports **aggregation windows** (daily/weekly/30d), **deduping**, and **suppression** (to reduce spam).
- Allows deterministic **scoring** + routing, with auditability.
- Minimizes sensitive data, aligning with the PLG signal data handling policy.

## Decision
Adopt a single canonical record called **PLG Signal (schema v1)** emitted by the scoring service and persisted in analytics storage. The router uses this object to:
- Update HubSpot company properties (latest score/type + history string)
- Create a HubSpot timeline note
- Create a follow-up task (when qualified)

The canonical record is **one row per (org, signal_type, window)** (with defined exceptions for multi-dimension signals described below).

## Definitions
- **Org**: Redwood customer organization (billing/account boundary). Primary entity for scoring and routing.
- **Workspace**: Sub-entity under an org; may exist for multi-team usage.
- **Signal Category**: 
  - **threshold** = derived from product usage / reliability / spend patterns
  - **intent** = derived from behavior that indicates buying evaluation (docs, security pages, pricing interactions)
- **Signal Type**: specific reason for outreach (e.g., `usage_tokens_sustained`, `reliability_429s_repeated`, `intent_private_docs_interest`).
